abstract:
Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have spurred interest in
deploying LLM agents to undertake tasks in the world. LLMs are often deployed
in agent systems: code that orchestrates LLM calls and provides them with
tools. We demonstrate that an agent system, equipped with basic coding tools,
can autonomously edit itself, and thereby improve its performance on benchmark
tasks. We find performance gains from 17% to 53% on a random subset of SWE
Bench Verified, with additional performance gains on LiveCodeBench, as well as
synthetically generated agent benchmarks. Our work represents an advancement in
the automated and open-ended design of agentic systems, and demonstrates a
data-efficient, non gradient-based learning mechanism driven by LLM reflection
and code updates.

keyInsights: A general-purpose coding agent can genuinely self-improve by editing its own Python codebase, demonstrating a fully self-referential system without a distinct meta-agent.
The system introduces a non-weight-based learning mechanism that relies on reflection and code updates, proving to be sample-efficient by learning from detailed inspection of past outcomes.
Automated agent design through self-modification reduces the reliance on manual, trial-and-error human efforts, offering a path to accelerate the development of complex agent systems.
originalProblem: Current agent systems often rely on ad-hoc, trial-and-error manual design for prompting schemes and orchestrators, which is labor-intensive and explores a limited solution space.
A need exists for AI systems that can learn and improve themselves through non-gradient-based, data-efficient mechanisms, distinct from traditional model weight updates.

Prior self-improving agent systems were either domain-specific, operated on fixed meta-agents, or lacked the general-purpose code editing capabilities required for arbitrary software engineering tasks.
results: SICA improved its accuracy on a random subset of 50 SWE-Bench Verified questions from 17% to 53% over 15 iterations.
The agent autonomously developed and integrated new tools, such as a 'Smart Editor' for efficient file editing and an 'AST Symbol Locator' for code navigation.
While incurring an API cost of approximately $7,000 for 15 iterations, the system also demonstrated slight decreases in average time spent per problem and improved token caching efficiency.
solution: A "Meta Agent Loop" is implemented where the best performing agent from an archive iteratively acts as a meta-agent to generate and evaluate new, improved agent versions.
The initial agent system is designed with foundational capabilities including a suite of coding and operational tools, and sub-agents for task decomposition (coding, problem-solving, reasoning).
An asynchronous LLM-based overseer monitors the agent's execution state, detects pathological behaviors, and can intervene or cancel execution to maintain task adherence and prevent resource wastage.
paper_summary.summary: A self-improving coding agent, SICA, autonomously edits its own Python codebase to enhance performance. This system achieved an improvement from 17% to 53% accuracy on SWE-Bench Verified tasks by developing new tools and refining its operational logic through a non-gradient-based learning mechanism.

title: A Self-Improving Coding Agent

abstract: Large language model (LLM) applications such as agents and domain-specific reasoning increasingly rely on context adaptation: modifying inputs with instructions, strategies, or evidence, rather than weight updates. Prior approaches improve usability but often suffer from brevity bias, which drops domain insights for concise summaries, and from context collapse, where iterative rewriting erodes details over time. We introduce ACE (Agentic Context Engineering), a framework that treats contexts as evolving playbooks that accumulate, refine, and organize strategies through a modular process of generation, reflection, and curation. ACE prevents collapse with structured, incremental updates that preserve detailed knowledge and scale with long-context models. Across agent and domain-specific benchmarks, ACE optimizes contexts both offline (e.g., system prompts) and online (e.g., agent memory), consistently outperforming strong baselines: +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance, while significantly reducing adaptation latency and rollout cost. Notably, ACE could adapt effectively without labeled supervision and instead by leveraging natural execution feedback. On the AppWorld leaderboard, ACE matches the top-ranked production-level agent on the overall average and surpasses it on the harder test-challenge split, despite using a smaller open-source model. These results show that comprehensive, evolving contexts enable scalable, efficient, and self-improving LLM systems with low overhead.

keyInsights: Treating contexts as dynamic, comprehensive "playbooks" rather than static, concise summaries allows for more robust and continuous LLM self-improvement.

Modularizing context adaptation into dedicated Generator, Reflector, and Curator components improves the quality and focus of context updates by separating generation from insight extraction and curation.

Incremental "delta updates" and a "grow-and-refine" mechanism are crucial for preventing context collapse and effectively preserving detailed knowledge over time, enabling scalable and efficient context evolution.
originalProblem: Existing context adaptation methods suffer from "brevity bias," where prompt optimization prioritizes conciseness, leading to the omission of critical domain-specific details, tool-use guidelines, or common failure modes.

"Context collapse" occurs when contexts are monolithically rewritten by an LLM, causing iterative degradation as accumulated information is compressed into shorter, less informative summaries, leading to performance declines.
Current methods hinder the performance and scalability of advanced LLM-based systems like autonomous agents and domain-specific reasoning tools.
results: ACE achieved average performance gains of 10.6% on agent benchmarks and 8.6% on domain-specific benchmarks compared to strong baselines, demonstrating improvements in both offline and online adaptation settings.

On the AppWorld benchmark, ReAct + ACE using DeepSeek-V3.1 matched or surpassed GPT-4.1-powered production agents (e.g., IBM-CUGA), with self-supervised adaptation showing an average improvement of 14.8% over the ReAct baseline.

The framework demonstrated significant reductions in adaptation latency (82.3% vs. GEPA, 91.5% vs. Dynamic Cheatsheet) and token costs (over 80% reduction), leveraging KV cache reuse to amortize the cost of longer contexts during evaluation.
solution: Introduces Agentic Context Engineering (ACE), a framework that treats contexts as dynamic, evolving "playbooks" that continuously accumulate, refine, and organize strategies.

Employs an agentic architecture with three specialized components: a Generator (produces reasoning trajectories), a Reflector (diagnoses errors and extracts insights), and a Curator (synthesizes insights into incremental "delta entries").

Utilizes "incremental delta updates" to represent context as itemized "bullets" and update only small, relevant parts, preventing full regeneration and preserving past knowledge through a "grow-and-refine" mechanism with de-duplication.
paper_summary.summary: Agentic Context Engineering (ACE) is a framework that enables large language models to self-improve by dynamically evolving comprehensive, 'playbook'-style contexts instead of static prompts. It consistently achieved average performance gains of over 8% across agentic and domain-specific tasks while reducing adaptation costs by more than 80% compared to baselines.

title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have been increasingly used to interact with
external environments (e.g., games, compilers, APIs) as goal-driven agents.
However, it remains challenging for these language agents to quickly and
efficiently learn from trial-and-error as traditional reinforcement learning
methods require extensive training samples and expensive model fine-tuning. We
propose Reflexion, a novel framework to reinforce language agents not by
updating weights, but instead through linguistic feedback. Concretely,
Reflexion agents verbally reflect on task feedback signals, then maintain their
own reflective text in an episodic memory buffer to induce better
decision-making in subsequent trials. Reflexion is flexible enough to
incorporate various types (scalar values or free-form language) and sources
(external or internally simulated) of feedback signals, and obtains significant
improvements over a baseline agent across diverse tasks (sequential
decision-making, coding, language reasoning). For example, Reflexion achieves a
91% pass@1 accuracy on the HumanEval coding benchmark, surpassing the previous
state-of-the-art GPT-4 that achieves 80%. We also conduct ablation and analysis
studies using different feedback signals, feedback incorporation methods, and
agent types, and provide insights into how they affect performance.

keyInsights: Converting numerical or binary environmental feedback into structured natural language "self-reflections" provides a more interpretable and actionable "semantic gradient signal" for LLMs.
Maintaining an explicit, persistent episodic memory of these verbal reflections allows LLM agents to accumulate experience and prevent repetitive errors over multiple trials.

LLMs possess an emergent ability for self-reflection that can be effectively harnessed to enable efficient, lightweight learning from failures in complex tasks like coding and sequential decision-making.
originalProblem: Existing large language model (LLM) agents struggle to learn from trial-and-error due to the prohibitive computational cost of traditional reinforcement learning (RL) for large models.
Current self-improvement methods for LLMs are often limited to single-generation reasoning or heuristic-based retries, lacking deeper learning from past mistakes over multiple episodes.
LLM agents primarily rely on static in-context examples, which restricts their adaptability and ability to recover from errors in dynamic environments.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art pass@1 accuracy on HumanEval (Python 91.0%, Rust 68.0%) and MBPP (Rust 75.4%), significantly surpassing GPT-4 baselines.

In sequential decision-making (ALFWorld), Reflexion completed 130 out of 134 tasks, demonstrating a 22% absolute improvement over the ReAct-only baseline by effectively mitigating common errors and adapting over trials.

Ablation studies confirmed that natural language self-reflection provides an additional 8% absolute boost in learning compared to just remembering raw trajectories on HotPotQA, and is essential for effective debugging in programming tasks.
solution: Reflexion proposes a "verbal reinforcement" paradigm where an LLM (Self-Reflection Model) generates natural language feedback from sparse environmental signals.

This free-form textual feedback is stored in a persistent episodic memory, which the primary LLM (Actor) uses as contextual hints to guide future decision-making without requiring model weight fine-tuning.

The framework integrates three distinct LLMs (Actor, Evaluator, Self-Reflection Model) in an iterative loop of trial, assessment, reflection, and memory update, enabling agents to learn from successive attempts.
paper_summary.summary: Reflexion enables large language model agents to learn from trial-and-error by converting environmental feedback into natural language reflections stored in an explicit episodic memory. This framework achieved new state-of-the-art pass@1 accuracy on several coding benchmarks and improved performance on sequential decision-making and language reasoning tasks.

title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Most Large Language Model (LLM) agent memory systems rely on a small set of static, hand-designed operations for extracting memory. These fixed procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long histories. To this end, we present \textbf{MemSkill}, which reframes these operations as learnable and evolvable memory skills, structured and reusable routines for extracting, consolidating, and pruning information from interaction traces. Inspired by the design philosophy of agent skills, MemSkill employs a \emph{controller} that learns to select a small set of relevant skills, paired with an LLM-based \emph{executor} that produces skill-guided memories. Beyond learning skill selection, MemSkill introduces a \emph{designer} that periodically reviews hard cases where selected skills yield incorrect or incomplete memories, and evolves the skill set by proposing refinements and new skills. Together, MemSkill forms a closed-loop procedure that improves both the skill-selection policy and the skill set itself. Experiments on LoCoMo, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, and ALFWorld demonstrate that MemSkill improves task performance over strong baselines and generalizes well across settings. Further analyses shed light on how skills evolve, offering insights toward more adaptive, self-evolving memory management for LLM agents.

keyInsights: Memory management for LLM agents can be conceptualized as a set of dynamic, reusable memory skills that can be learned and refined over time.
A continuous feedback loop, where an agent learns to utilize skills and a designer periodically evolves these skills based on performance, leads to self-improving memory systems.
Explicitly structuring memory behaviors into an interpretable skill bank promotes adaptability, reusability across tasks, and offers a pathway towards more autonomous LLM agents.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems rely on static, human-engineered operations, which are rigid and inefficient for long, open-ended interactions.
The hard-coded nature of memory extraction, consolidation, and pruning limits adaptability to diverse interaction patterns and hinders scalability over growing interaction histories.
Prior methods primarily optimize *how to use* a predefined set of memory operations rather than enabling the evolution of the operations themselves.
results: MemSkill consistently achieved superior performance, obtaining the highest LLM-judge scores on conversational benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval) and the highest success rates on embodied interactive tasks (ALFWorld) compared to strong baselines.

The system demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, with learned skills transferring effectively across different base LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-3 to Qwen3) and diverse datasets (e.g., LoCoMo to HotpotQA) without requiring retraining.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the learned skill selection by the controller and the skill evolution process orchestrated by the designer are critical components contributing to MemSkill's performance gains.
solution: MemSkill reframes memory operations as "learnable and evolvable memory skills" stored in a dynamic "skill bank."
It employs a closed-loop optimization recipe combining reinforcement learning for a controller to select relevant skills and an LLM-guided designer to evolve the skill bank from hard cases.
A fixed LLM executor constructs skill-guided memories for text spans by conditioning on selected skills, generating structured memory updates in a single pass.
paper_summary.summary: MemSkill introduces an approach for Large Language Model (LLM) agents where memory operations are learned and evolve over time, moving beyond static, human-engineered mechanisms. This system, developed by Nanyang Technological University and collaborators, achieves superior performance in conversational and embodied tasks by continually refining a dynamic skill bank and a skill-selection policy.

title: MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: The performance of large language models (LLMs) depends on how they are prompted, with choices spanning both the high-level prompting pattern (e.g., Zero-Shot, CoT, ReAct, ReWOO) and the specific prompt content (instructions and few-shot demonstrations). Manually tuning this combination is tedious, error-prone, and specific to a given LLM and task. Therefore, this paper proposes AutoPDL, an automated approach to discovering good LLM agent configurations. Our approach frames this as a structured AutoML problem over a combinatorial space of agentic and non-agentic prompting patterns and demonstrations, using successive halving to efficiently navigate this space. We introduce a library implementing common prompting patterns using the PDL prompt programming language. AutoPDL solutions are human-readable, editable, and executable PDL programs that use this library. This approach also enables source-to-source optimization, allowing human-in-the-loop refinement and reuse. Evaluations across three tasks and seven LLMs (ranging from 3B to 70B parameters) show consistent accuracy gains ($9.21\pm15.46$ percentage points), up to 67.5pp, and reveal that selected prompting strategies vary across models and tasks.

keyInsights: Optimal prompting strategies vary significantly across different LLMs and tasks, confirming that no single 'best' prompt or pattern exists for universal application.
Prompt programs optimized on smaller, open-source models can effectively transfer to more powerful, expensive frontier models, suggesting substantial cost savings in development.
The 'source-to-source' optimization approach, yielding human-readable PDL code, enhances transparency, debuggability, and developer control over LLM agent behavior.
originalProblem: Manually tuning prompts for LLMs and complex agents is a time-consuming, error-prone process requiring significant expertise.
Optimal prompt configurations are highly specific to the LLM, task, and dataset, leading to a lack of generalizability.
Existing prompt optimization methods often focus on isolated components or produce opaque, non-human-readable outputs, hindering debugging and further refinement.
results: AutoPDL consistently improved LLM accuracy over zero-shot baselines by an average of 9.21    15.46 percentage points, with gains reaching up to 67.5pp in certain cases.
The optimal prompting patterns varied by task, with CoT frequently chosen for math problems (GSM8K) and ReAct often optimal for code generation (MBPP+) due to its iterative reasoning and tool use.
Optimized PDL programs from open-source models successfully transferred to OpenAI's GPT-4o-mini, yielding performance improvements of 4.0pp on FEVER, 9.3pp on GSM-Hard, and 13.1pp on GSM8K.
solution: AutoPDL frames prompt optimization as a structured AutoML problem, searching a combinatorial space of high-level prompting patterns (e.g., CoT, ReAct) and specific prompt content (instructions, few-shot examples).

It employs the successive halving algorithm to efficiently explore this vast search space, systematically pruning less promising candidates and focusing computational resources on effective configurations.
The system uses PDL (Prompt Declaration Language) to represent both initial specifications and optimized prompt programs, ensuring outputs are human-readable, editable, and directly executable.
paper_summary.summary: AutoPDL automates the optimization of prompting patterns and content for LLM agents, generating human-readable configurations. This framework achieved an average accuracy gain of 9.21 percentage points across diverse tasks and models, and demonstrated effective transferability to commercial frontier models.

title: AutoPDL: Automatic Prompt Optimization for LLM Agents

abstract: The operational efficacy of large language models relies heavily on their inference-time context. This has established Context Engineering (CE) as a formal discipline for optimizing these inputs. Current CE methods rely on manually crafted harnesses, such as rigid generation-reflection workflows and predefined context schemas. They impose structural biases and restrict context optimization to a narrow, intuition-bound design space. To address this, we introduce Meta Context Engineering (MCE), a bi-level framework that supersedes static CE heuristics by co-evolving CE skills and context artifacts. In MCE iterations, a meta-level agent refines engineering skills via agentic crossover, a deliberative search over the history of skills, their executions, and evaluations. A base-level agent executes these skills, learns from training rollouts, and optimizes context as flexible files and code. We evaluate MCE across five disparate domains under offline and online settings. MCE demonstrates consistent performance gains, achieving 5.6--53.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art agentic CE methods (mean of 16.9%), while maintaining superior context adaptability, transferability, and efficiency in both context usage and training.

keyInsights: Context Engineering can be formulated as a bi-level optimization problem, allowing for the co-evolution of context generation strategies (skills) and the resulting context artifacts.
"Agentic crossover" provides a flexible and deliberative mechanism for a meta-level agent to synthesize superior context engineering skills by analyzing past performance and task requirements.
Representing context as arbitrary files and code, rather than rigid schemas, enables fully agentic context optimization at the base-level, free from structural biases.
originalProblem: Existing Context Engineering (CE) methods rely on manually crafted "agentic harnesses" that introduce structural biases and rigid context representations.
Current approaches constrain context optimization to an "intuition-bound design space," limiting the discovery of truly task-optimal strategies.
Static CE heuristics result in contexts that lack adaptability, token efficiency, and transferability across various LLM capacities and task complexities.
results: MCE consistently outperformed state-of-the-art context engineering methods, achieving an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models in offline settings and 74.1% in online settings.
The framework demonstrated superior context efficiency, achieving higher performance with significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 75% accuracy with 20K tokens on FiNER vs. 70% with 79K for ACE).
MCE substantially reduced training duration by 13.6x and required 4.8x fewer rollouts compared to leading baselines, while also enhancing context transferability between LLMs.
solution: MCE introduces a bi-level optimization framework that enables the co-evolution of Context Engineering (CE) "skills" (strategies) and context "artifacts."
A meta-level agent iteratively refines CE skills using "agentic crossover," synthesizing superior strategies by reasoning over historical data.
A base-level agent executes these evolved skills to construct and optimize context, represented as flexible files and code, using coding toolkits and file system access.
paper_summary.summary: Meta Context Engineering (MCE) enables large language model agents to autonomously co-evolve their context engineering strategies (skills) and context artifacts through a bi-level optimization framework. This method enhances performance and efficiency by dynamically adapting context properties, yielding an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models and outperforming state-of-the-art context engineering methods.

title: Meta Context Engineering via Agentic Skill Evolution

abstract:
Increasing test-time compute for LLMs shows promise across domains but
remains underexplored in code generation, despite extensive study in math. In
this paper, we propose S*, the first hybrid test-time scaling framework that
substantially improves the coverage and selection accuracy of generated code.
S* extends the existing parallel scaling paradigm with sequential scaling to
push performance boundaries. It further leverages a novel selection mechanism
that adaptively generates distinguishing inputs for pairwise comparison,
combined with execution-grounded information to robustly identify correct
solutions. We evaluate across 12 Large Language Models and Large Reasoning
Model and show: (1) S* consistently improves performance across model families
and sizes, enabling a 3B model to outperform GPT-4o-mini; (2) S* enables
non-reasoning models to surpass reasoning models - GPT-4o-mini with S*
outperforms o1-preview by 3.7% on LiveCodeBench; (3) S* further boosts
state-of-the-art reasoning models - DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B with S*
achieves 85.7% on LiveCodeBench, approaching o1 (high) at 88.5%. Code will be
available under this https URL

keyInsights: Hybrid approach combining parallel and sequential scaling is more effective than either alone
Execution feedback and test case generation are crucial for reliable solution selection
Non-reasoning models can outperform reasoning models when using appropriate test-time scaling
originalProblem: Existing test-time scaling approaches focus mainly on mathematical reasoning, leaving code generation underexplored
Code generation requires both high coverage (solving diverse problems) and accurate solution selection
results: Consistent improvements across 12 different LLMs of varying sizes
3B parameter model outperformed GPT-4o-mini using S*
Achieved 85.7% accuracy on LiveCodeBench with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
Demonstrated effectiveness on both LiveCodeBench and CodeContests benchmarks
solution: Two-stage framework combining parallel sampling with sequential debugging
Novel adaptive input synthesis for robust solution comparison and selection
Execution-grounded feedback loop for iterative code refinement
paper_summary.summary: UC Berkeley researchers introduce S*, a breakthrough hybrid test-time scaling framework that enables dramatic improvements in code generation performance across model sizes, allowing a 3B parameter model to outperform GPT-4o-mini through clever combination of parallel sampling and sequential debugging while achieving 85.7% accuracy on LiveCodeBench with larger models.

title: S*: Test Time Scaling for Code Generation

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) deployed as agents solve user-specified tasks
over multiple steps while keeping the required manual engagement to a minimum.
Crucially, such LLMs need to ground their generations in any feedback obtained
to reliably achieve the desired outcomes. We propose an end-to-end
reinforcement learning method for teaching models to leverage execution
feedback in the realm of code synthesis, where state-of-the-art LLMs struggle
to improve code iteratively compared to independent sampling. We benchmark on
competitive programming tasks, where we achieve new state-of-the art results
with both small (8B parameters) and large (70B) models while reducing the
amount of samples required by an order of magnitude. Our analysis of
inference-time behavior demonstrates that our method produces LLMs that
effectively leverage automatic feedback over multiple steps.

keyInsights: RLEF successfully teaches LLMs to interpret and leverage textual execution feedback for iterative code repair, showing improved recovery from all error categories.
The fine-tuned models learn to make larger and more targeted code edits based on error messages, unlike base LLMs which often repeat previous solutions or make minimal changes.

Training LLMs with an end-to-end multi-turn RL approach, specifically RLEF, is found to be more effective for acquiring iterative code synthesis capabilities than supervised fine-tuning or few-shot prompting.
originalProblem: Despite strong initial code generation, state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle with iterative improvement based on execution feedback, frequently performing worse than simply sampling independent solutions.
Sophisticated 'agentic frameworks' for code generation achieve high performance but incur significantly increased inference costs due to numerous LLM calls and complex manual scaffolding.
Current LLMs lack robust 'grounding' capabilities, meaning they do not effectively integrate dynamic, inference-time feedback to refine their outputs and reliably achieve desired outcomes.
results: The Llama 3.1 70B + RLEF model achieves a 1@3 solve rate of 40.1% on CodeContests, surpassing AlphaCodium with GPT-4 (29% for 5@100 samples) and demonstrating higher accuracy with an order of magnitude fewer samples.

RLEF-trained models consistently benefit from execution feedback, exhibiting significantly improved recovery from errors and outperforming base LLMs (including GPT-4o) in multi-turn iterative code generation.

Ablation studies confirm that providing meaningful execution feedback during training is critical for self-repair, and the chosen turn-level value function performs better than a token-level value function.
solution: The paper introduces Reinforcement Learning with Execution Feedback (RLEF), an end-to-end fine-tuning approach that frames code synthesis as an iterative, multi-turn reinforcement learning problem.
Code solutions are automatically evaluated against public tests, with the results (errors, passed tests) formatted as textual 'execution feedback' and appended to the dialog history as observations.

Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is used to fine-tune Llama 3.1 models, optimizing a scalar reward function that incentivizes correct solutions and penalizes errors, along with a hybrid turn-level value function.
paper_summary.summary: RLEF by Meta AI (FAIR) enhances Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to self-correct code using execution feedback via reinforcement learning. This approach achieves state-of-the-art code generation solve rates on CodeContests with an order of magnitude fewer samples than previous methods, significantly improving efficiency and reliability.

title: RLEF: Grounding Code LLMs in Execution Feedback with Reinforcement  Learning

abstract: Recent advances demonstrate that increasing inference-time computation can significantly boost the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Although repeated sampling (i.e., generating multiple candidate outputs) is a highly effective strategy, it does not leverage external feedback signals for refinement, which are often available in tasks like coding. In this work, we propose Adaptive Branching Monte Carlo Tree Search (AB-MCTS), a novel inference-time framework that generalizes repeated sampling with principled multi-turn exploration and exploitation. At each node in the search tree, AB-MCTS dynamically decides whether to "go wider" by expanding new candidate responses or "go deeper" by revisiting existing ones based on external feedback signals. We evaluate our method on complex coding and engineering tasks using frontier models. Empirical results show that AB-MCTS consistently outperforms both repeated sampling and standard MCTS, underscoring the importance of combining the response diversity of LLMs with multi-turn solution refinement for effective inference-time scaling. Code is available at this https URL .

keyInsights: Bayesian decision-making through Thompson Sampling provides a principled way to dynamically balance exploration (generating new solutions) and exploitation (refining existing solutions) in LLM-driven tree search.
Allowing 'unbounded branching' via specific node types (e.g., GEN nodes) is crucial for leveraging the full diversity of LLM outputs, which fixed-width tree search methods cannot achieve.

The ability to adapt search strategy based on external feedback enables more robust performance across varied tasks, outperforming static or purely explorative methods that may struggle with specific problem structures.
originalProblem: Existing LLM inference-time scaling methods, such as repeated sampling, primarily focus on exploration without a principled mechanism for exploiting or refining promising solutions based on external feedback.
Traditional Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) approaches for LLMs typically use a fixed branching factor, limiting the search's ability to fully capitalize on the vast and diverse output space of LLMs.

There is a need for an adaptive method that can intelligently allocate LLM computational resources by dynamically deciding whether to explore new candidates ('go wider') or refine existing ones ('go deeper') during complex problem-solving.
results: AB-MCTS variants consistently achieved higher average ranks across competitive programming (LiveCodeBench, CodeContest), abstract reasoning (ARC-AGI), and ML engineering (MLE-Bench) benchmarks compared to baselines, utilizing GPT-4o and DeepSeek-V3.
The method demonstrated more efficient use of LLM inference budgets, reaching peak performance earlier or achieving superior ultimate success rates, particularly as computational budgets increased.

Analysis revealed that AB-MCTS generally generates wider search trees compared to standard MCTS, demonstrating flexible exploration while simultaneously excelling on tasks that benefit from sequential refinement, showcasing its adaptive balance of width and depth.
solution: The Adaptive Branching Monte Carlo Tree Search (AB-MCTS) framework was developed, generalizing repeated sampling by integrating multi-turn exploration and exploitation within an MCTS structure.

Special 'GEN nodes' are introduced to enable 'unbounded branching,' allowing dynamic generation of new child nodes, while Thompson Sampling is used with Bayesian posterior updates to adaptively guide the search strategy (wider vs. deeper).

Two variants, AB-MCTS-M (Mixed Model) and AB-MCTS-A (Node Aggregation), are proposed to model and update the posterior predictive distributions for decision-making, with AB-MCTS-A offering a computationally lighter alternative using exponential family distributions.
paper_summary.summary: An inference-time framework named Adaptive Branching Monte Carlo Tree Search (AB-MCTS) was introduced by Sakana AI to dynamically balance exploration and exploitation in large language model problem-solving. This method improved performance on complex coding, abstract reasoning, and machine learning engineering tasks, demonstrating more effective utilization of LLM inference budgets compared to existing search strategies.

title: Wider or Deeper? Scaling LLM Inference-Time Compute with Adaptive Branching Tree Search

abstract:
Inference-time techniques, such as repeated sampling or iterative revisions,
are emerging as powerful ways to enhance large-language models (LLMs) at test
time. However, best practices for developing systems that combine these
techniques remain underdeveloped due to our limited understanding of the
utility of each technique across models and tasks, the interactions between
them, and the massive search space for combining them. To address these
challenges, we introduce Archon, a modular and automated framework for
optimizing the process of selecting and combining inference-time techniques and
LLMs. Given a compute budget and a set of available LLMs, Archon explores a
large design space to discover optimized configurations tailored to target
benchmarks. It can design custom or general-purpose architectures that advance
the Pareto frontier of accuracy vs. maximum token budget compared to
top-performing baselines. Across instruction-following, reasoning, and coding
tasks, we show that Archon can leverage additional inference compute budget to
design systems that outperform frontier models such as OpenAI's o1, GPT-4o, and
Claude 3.5 Sonnet by an average of 15.1%.

keyInsights: Individual inference-time techniques like repeated sampling, ensembling, and fusion consistently provide substantial performance gains, with ensembling boosting performance by 18.5% and fusion by 8.9% on average.
Synergistic combinations of techniques, such as applying a Critic and Ranker before a Fuser, often yield greater performance benefits than the sum of their individual parts.

Architectures discovered by ARCHON can be optimized for specific tasks, achieving higher accuracy, or generalized across multiple tasks, retaining 91-94% of task-specific performance on unseen benchmarks.
originalProblem: Practitioners manually select and combine large language models (LLMs) with inference-time techniques, leading to sub-optimal or domain-specific solutions.
There is a limited understanding of the individual utility and synergistic interactions of various inference-time techniques (e.g., ensembling, critiquing, fusion) for different tasks.
The design space for creating robust LLM inference systems is expansive, making manual exploration inefficient and prone to error.
results: ARCHON architectures achieve an average of 15.1% performance improvement over frontier LLMs (e.g., o1, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and existing inference-time architectures.
The framework demonstrates superior compute efficiency, being 20.0% more inference call efficient and using 15.1% fewer input tokens compared to other open-source inference-time frameworks.
Specific techniques show strong impact, such as the Unit Test Generator/Evaluator boosting CodeContests Pass@1 performance by 56% (from 17.9% to 29.3%).
solution: ARCHON provides a modular framework composed of distinct LLM components (e.g., Generator, Fuser, Critic, Verifier), each implementing a specific inference-time technique.
It defines clear rules for combining these components into multi-layered inference architectures, reminiscent of neural networks, but transforming state sequentially.

The framework utilizes Bayesian Optimization to efficiently search a vast design space of architecture configurations, automating the discovery of high-performing LLM inference systems under specified compute constraints.
paper_summary.summary: ARCHON presents an automated architecture search framework to systematically design and optimize large language model inference systems by combining various inference-time techniques. This framework achieves an average 15.1% performance improvement over frontier models like GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, while also demonstrating 20.0% greater inference call efficiency.

title: Archon: An Architecture Search Framework for Inference-Time Techniques

abstract: Memory systems have been designed to leverage past experiences in Large Language Model (LLM) agents. However, many deployed memory systems primarily optimize compression and storage, with comparatively less emphasis on explicit, closed-loop control of memory retrieval. From this observation, we build memory retrieval as an autonomous, accurate, and compatible agent system, named MemR$^3$, which has two core mechanisms: 1) a router that selects among retrieve, reflect, and answer actions to optimize answer quality; 2) a global evidence-gap tracker that explicitly renders the answering process transparent and tracks the evidence collection process. This design departs from the standard retrieve-then-answer pipeline by introducing a closed-loop control mechanism that enables autonomous decision-making. Empirical results on the LoCoMo benchmark demonstrate that MemR$^3$ surpasses strong baselines on LLM-as-a-Judge score, and particularly, it improves existing retrievers across four categories with an overall improvement on RAG (+7.29%) and Zep (+1.94%) using GPT-4.1-mini backend, offering a plug-and-play controller for existing memory stores.

keyInsights: Maintaining an explicit "evidence-gap" state allows agents to reason under partial observability and dynamically refine their information-seeking strategy.
Transforming memory retrieval into a closed-loop, autonomous sequential decision process significantly enhances the accuracy and efficiency of LLM agents.
A plug-and-play retrieval controller can be integrated with diverse memory backends (e.g., RAG, Zep) to improve retrieval quality without modifying storage architecture.
originalProblem: Non-parametric memory systems for LLM agents often rely on heuristic, open-loop retrieval, leading to noisy recall, excessive context, and latency issues.
LLM agents lack explicit, closed-loop control mechanisms for memory retrieval, limiting their ability to intelligently gather specific information.
Managing and leveraging long-term, personalized memories remains a significant challenge for sophisticated AI agents.
results: MemR^3 improved LLM-as-a-Judge (J) scores by up to 7.29% (RAG-backed, GPT-4.1-mini) on the LoCoMo benchmark, consistently outperforming its base retrievers and baselines.
The system demonstrated substantial gains across multi-hop, temporal, and open-domain questions, approaching or surpassing full-context performance in several categories.
Ablation studies highlighted the crucial role of "masked retrieval" (preventing redundant retrieval) and the positive impact of explicit reflection and query refinement.
solution: MemR^3 frames memory retrieval as an autonomous, sequential decision process within a LangGraph-based agent graph.
It introduces a global evidence-gap tracker (E, G) to explicitly monitor known facts and remaining informational needs, guiding retrieval.
A Router node provides closed-loop control, dynamically orchestrating actions between Retrieve, Reflect, and Answer nodes to refine knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: MemR^3 introduces a system for LLM agents that uses reflective reasoning and an explicit evidence-gap tracker to enable closed-loop control over memory retrieval. This approach consistently enhanced answer quality, achieving up to a 7.29% improvement in LLM-as-a-Judge scores on the LoCoMo benchmark compared to traditional RAG.

title: MemR$^3$: Memory Retrieval via Reflective Reasoning for LLM Agents

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in environments that generate massive, dynamic contexts. However, a critical bottleneck remains: while agents have access to this context, their static prompts lack the mechanisms to manage it effectively, leading to recurring Corrective and Enhancement failures. To address this capability gap, we introduce \textbf{SCOPE} (Self-evolving Context Optimization via Prompt Evolution). SCOPE frames context management as an \textit{online optimization} problem, synthesizing guidelines from execution traces to automatically evolve the agent's prompt. We propose a Dual-Stream mechanism that balances tactical specificity (resolving immediate errors) with strategic generality (evolving long-term principles). Furthermore, we introduce Perspective-Driven Exploration to maximize strategy coverage, increasing the likelihood that the agent has the correct strategy for any given task. Experiments on the HLE benchmark show that SCOPE improves task success rates from 14.23\% to 38.64\% without human intervention. We make our code publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Treating the agent's prompt as an evolvable parameter that learns online from execution traces enables step-level adaptation and per-agent optimization.
Placing evolved guidelines within the system prompt rather than the user prompt fosters 'implicit background guidance,' leading to better overall task success.
Leveraging multiple 'Perspectives' through parallel prompt evolution streams allows agents to learn diverse, complementary strategies, significantly enhancing overall performance.
originalProblem: LLM agents struggle with ineffective context management, often getting 'flooded with context' but failing to extract actionable signals due to static prompts.
Agents exhibit prevalent failure modes, including 'Corrective Failures' (treating explicit errors as generic alarms) and 'Enhancement Failures' (persisting with suboptimal strategies).
Existing approaches offer limited real-time adaptation, task-level granularity, or static prompts during inference, falling short in complex, dynamic environments.
results: SCOPE achieves state-of-the-art performance, increasing task success rates from 14.23% to 38.64% on HLE and from 32.73% to 56.97% on GAIA.
Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each SCOPE component, with Perspective-Driven Exploration yielding the largest individual gain (+10.91% on GAIA).

The framework's performance is robust to the choice of LLM for its meta-agent components, indicating that guideline quality through selection and optimization is more critical than the quantity of generated guidelines.
solution: SCOPE introduces a 'prompt evolution' paradigm, treating the agent's prompt as an evolvable parameter that updates online based on its execution traces.
It synthesizes natural language 'guidelines' from execution traces (corrective for errors, enhancement for suboptimality) via a Generator and selects the best using a Selector.

A dual-stream routing mechanism classifies guidelines into tactical (task-specific) and strategic (long-term) memory, which are then integrated into the agent's prompt, while Perspective-Driven Exploration uses parallel streams to learn diverse strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The paper introduces SCOPE, a framework enabling large language model agents to dynamically evolve their prompts online by learning from their execution traces. This approach leads to a substantial increase in task success rates, with the HLE benchmark showing an improvement from 14.23% to 38.64%, and GAIA from 32.73% to 56.97%.

title: SCOPE: Prompt Evolution for Enhancing Agent Effectiveness

abstract: Agents based on large language models (LLMs) for machine learning engineering (MLE) can automatically implement ML models via code generation. However, existing approaches to build such agents often rely heavily on inherent LLM knowledge and employ coarse exploration strategies that modify the entire code structure at once. This limits their ability to select effective task-specific models and perform deep exploration within specific components, such as experimenting extensively with feature engineering options. To overcome these, we propose MLE-STAR, a novel approach to build MLE agents. MLE-STAR first leverages external knowledge by using a search engine to retrieve effective models from the web, forming an initial solution, then iteratively refines it by exploring various strategies targeting specific ML components. This exploration is guided by ablation studies analyzing the impact of individual code blocks. Furthermore, we introduce a novel ensembling method using an effective strategy suggested by MLE-STAR. Our experimental results show that MLE-STAR achieves medals in 64% of the Kaggle competitions on the MLE-bench Lite, significantly outperforming the best alternative.

keyInsights: Integrating external web search allows LLM agents to overcome knowledge biases and dynamically incorporate up-to-date, task-specific ML models and practices.
Focusing refinement efforts on specific, high-impact code blocks identified through ablation studies leads to more efficient and effective performance gains.
Proactive detection and correction of common LLM code misbehaviors (like data leakage) are critical for generating robust and reliable ML solutions.
Intelligent, LLM-guided ensembling can further boost model performance by effectively combining diverse candidate solutions.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based ML agents often propose familiar or outdated ML methods due to their training data, limiting the use of state-of-the-art approaches.
Current agents perform coarse, broad modifications to code, hindering deep and iterative optimization of specific ML pipeline components.
Many LLM agents face scalability and generalizability issues due to reliance on manually curated knowledge bases or case banks.
LLM-generated code can exhibit 'misbehaviors' like data leakage or incomplete data utilization, compromising solution robustness.
results: Achieved a 63.6% medal rate (36.4% gold medals) on 22 Kaggle competitions within the MLE-bench Lite benchmark using Gemini-2.5-Pro.
Outperformed the leading LLM agent, AIDE, by 18.1 percentage points in 'Any Medal' rate (43.9% vs 25.8%) using the same Gemini-2.0-Flash model.
Demonstrated superior performance against DS-Agent and AutoGluon on most tabular tasks, without relying on manually curated case banks.
The proposed ensembling method significantly improved the 'Any Medal' rate from 37.9% to 43.9% and the 'Gold Medal' rate from 25.8% to 30.3%.
solution: A multi-agent framework that uses a retriever agent with web search to identify state-of-the-art ML models and example code relevant to a given task.
A targeted code block refinement mechanism where an extractor agent, guided by ablation studies, identifies and iteratively refines high-impact ML components.
A novel, LLM-guided ensemble strategy that proposes and evaluates various ensemble plans to combine multiple candidate solutions.
Auxiliary modules for debugging, data leakage checking, and data usage checking to ensure the robustness and correctness of generated ML solutions.
paper_summary.summary: MLE-STAR is an LLM-based agent that automates machine learning engineering tasks by leveraging web search for up-to-date models and implementing a targeted, ablation-guided code refinement process. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on a benchmark of 22 Kaggle competitions, outperforming existing LLM agents and specialized AutoML tools.

title: MLE-STAR: Machine Learning Engineering Agent via Search and Targeted Refinement

abstract: Despite significant strides in language models in the last few years, when used as agents, such models often try to perform actions that are not just suboptimal for a given state, but are strictly prohibited by the external environment. For example, in the recent Kaggle GameArena chess competition, 78% of Gemini-2.5-Flash losses were attributed to illegal moves. Often people manually write "harnesses" around LLMs to prevent such failures. In this paper, we demonstrate that Gemini-2.5-Flash can automatically synthesize such a code harness, using a small number of rounds of iterative code refinement given feedback from the (game) environment. The resulting harness prevents all illegal moves in 145 different TextArena games (both 1-player and 2-player), enabling the smaller Gemini-2.5-Flash model to outperform larger models, such as Gemini-2.5-Pro. Pushing our technique to the limit, we can get Gemini-2.5-Flash to generate the entire policy in code, thus eliminating the need to use the LLM at decision making time. The resulting code-policy receives a higher average reward than Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5.2-High on 16 TextArena 1-player games. Our results show that using a smaller model to synthesize a custom code harness (or entire policy) can outperform a much larger model, while also being more cost effective.

keyInsights: LLMs possess the capability to autonomously generate robust, game-specific code logic that effectively enforces environmental rules, addressing their inherent brittleness.
Externalizing action validation into verifiable code significantly enhances an agent's reliability and enables smaller, more cost-effective LLMs to outperform larger models.

Generating entire policies as executable code offers dramatically reduced inference costs and superior performance for tasks, shifting LLM utility from runtime decision-making to compile-time code synthesis.
originalProblem: Large language model (LLM) agents frequently fail to adhere to environmental rules, leading to illegal actions and brittle behavior in complex tasks.
Existing solutions like fine-tuning LLMs are computationally expensive and time-consuming, while manual code harnesses are labor-intensive and not scalable.
This fundamental disconnect between an LLM's strategic reasoning and its ability to execute valid actions hinders reliable agent deployment.
results: AutoHarness successfully synthesized code harnesses that achieved a 100% legal action success rate across all 145 evaluated TextArena games.
A smaller Gemini-2.5-Flash model, when augmented with the generated action-verifier harness, achieved a 56.3% win rate against Gemini-2.5-Pro in two-player games, surpassing its performance.

The 'harness-as-policy' variant yielded the highest average reward of 0.870 in one-player games and incurred 'nearly zero' test-time computational costs, significantly outperforming other LLM-based agents.
solution: The framework enables an LLM (Refiner) to automatically synthesize auxiliary Python code, referred to as a 'harness,' which either verifies proposed actions or directly encodes the entire game policy.
An iterative refinement process, guided by environmental feedback and error messages, is employed within a tree search algorithm utilizing Thompson sampling to efficiently explore code variations.

Two primary harness types are developed: 'harness-as-action-verifier' which filters LLM-proposed actions, and 'harness-as-policy' which encapsulates the full game-playing logic in generated Python code.
paper_summary.summary: AutoHarness, developed at Google DeepMind, introduces a framework where large language models autonomously generate code harnesses to prevent illegal actions in rule-based environments. This approach enabled 100% legal action adherence across 145 text games and allowed smaller LLMs to outperform larger models, with the 'harness-as-policy' variant achieving a 0.870 average reward and near-zero inference costs.

title: AutoHarness: improving LLM agents by automatically synthesizing a code harness

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across
a wide range of tasks. To further tailor LLMs to specific domains or
applications, post-training techniques such as Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT),
Preference Learning, and model merging are commonly employed. While each of
these methods has been extensively studied in isolation, the automated
construction of complete post-training pipelines remains an underexplored area.
Existing approaches typically rely on manual design or focus narrowly on
optimizing individual components, such as data ordering or merging strategies.
In this work, we introduce LaMDAgent (short for Language Model Developing
Agent), a novel framework that autonomously constructs and optimizes full
post-training pipelines through the use of LLM-based agents. LaMDAgent
systematically explores diverse model generation techniques, datasets, and
hyperparameter configurations, leveraging task-based feedback to discover
high-performing pipelines with minimal human intervention. Our experiments show
that LaMDAgent improves tool-use accuracy by 9.0 points while preserving
instruction-following capabilities. Moreover, it uncovers effective
post-training strategies that are often overlooked by conventional human-driven
exploration. We further analyze the impact of data and model size scaling to
reduce computational costs on the exploration, finding that model size scalings
introduces new challenges, whereas scaling data size enables cost-effective
pipeline discovery.

keyInsights: Simple supervised fine-tuning on all data can outperform specialist model merging in multi-skill settings, contradicting some prior assumptions
High-performing pipelines often involve counter-intuitive sequences like merging models before final fine-tuning steps
Data size scaling transfers effectively across pipeline configurations, enabling cost-efficient exploration on smaller datasets
originalProblem: Current LLM post-training adaptation relies heavily on manual design and expert heuristics, requiring extensive trial-and-error experimentation
Existing approaches optimize individual components (data ordering, model merging) in isolation rather than complete end-to-end pipelines
Manual exploration struggles with the combinatorial complexity of sequencing multiple post-training techniques and often misses non-intuitive strategies
results: Multi-skill enhancement: 1.9-point overall improvement with 3.7-point gain in arithmetic reasoning while maintaining other capabilities
Tool usage improvement: 9.0-point AceBench accuracy increase (0.410 to 0.500) while preserving MT-Bench instruction-following performance
Ablation studies confirm critical role of LLM agent (1.9-point drop with random selection) and importance of comprehensive action spaces
solution: Autonomous framework with four-step iterative loop: action enumeration, LLM agent-based action selection, model evaluation, and memory update
Unified representation of heterogeneous model improvement methods (SFT, TIES-Merging) as "actions" operating on dynamic object pools containing models and datasets
LLM agent (gpt-4o) guides exploration using memory of past experiences and exploration directives to prevent mode collapse
paper_summary.summary: NEC Corporation researchers develop LaMDAgent, an autonomous framework that uses LLM-based agents to construct and optimize complete post-training pipelines for large language models, achieving 1.9-point improvement over strong baselines in multi-skill learning and 9.0-point improvement in tool usage while preserving instruction-following capabilities through iterative feedback loops that systematically explore combinations of supervised fine-tuning and model merging strategies, discovering non-obvious high-performing pipelines such as merging models before final fine-tuning steps that human-designed approaches typically overlook.

title: LaMDAgent: An Autonomous Framework for Post-Training Pipeline  Optimization via LLM Agents

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown significant capability across various
tasks, with their real-world effectiveness often driven by prompt design. While
recent research has focused on optimizing prompt content, the role of prompt
formatting, a critical but often overlooked dimension, has received limited
systematic investigation. In this paper, we introduce Content-Format Integrated
Prompt Optimization (CFPO), an innovative methodology that jointly optimizes
both prompt content and formatting through an iterative refinement process.
CFPO leverages natural language mutations to explore content variations and
employs a dynamic format exploration strategy that systematically evaluates
diverse format options. Our extensive evaluations across multiple tasks and
open-source LLMs demonstrate that CFPO demonstrates measurable performance
improvements compared to content-only optimization methods. This highlights the
importance of integrated content-format optimization and offers a practical,
model-agnostic approach to enhancing LLM performance. Code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Prompt formatting is a critical, optimizable dimension that deeply interacts with content, and their joint optimization is essential for maximizing LLM performance across various tasks.
An LLM-assisted mechanism for dynamically generating new format descriptions and corresponding code can effectively expand the search space and discover superior formatting strategies.
Incorporating both correct and incorrect cases during the diagnostic phase of content optimization significantly enhances refinement efficacy compared to focusing solely on errors.
originalProblem: Existing automated prompt optimization techniques primarily focus on refining the semantic content of prompts, often neglecting how that content is presented.
Prompt formatting, including arrangement of components, use of separators, or capitalization, can significantly impact LLM performance, yet current practices lack systematic design principles.
The effectiveness of a prompt depends on the complex interaction between its content and format, making a one-size-fits-all approach or isolated optimization suboptimal.
results: CFPO consistently achieved superior performance across diverse tasks and open-source LLMs (e.g., Mistral-7B-v0.1, LLaMA-3.1-8B), outperforming baseline and state-of-the-art content-only methods.
It demonstrated notable gains in complex reasoning tasks, boosting Mistral-7B-v0.1 accuracy on GSM8K from 36.85% to 53.22%, and LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct from 74.00% to 80.74%.

Ablation studies confirmed that the full CFPO pipeline, encompassing both joint content-format optimization and LLM-assisted format generation, is crucial for optimal results, outperforming content-only or sequential optimization variants.
solution: CFPO proposes an iterative framework that concurrently optimizes both prompt content and formatting through a dual-optimizer approach.

It utilizes a structured prompt template that decomposes prompts into distinct content-based (e.g., Task Instruction) and format-based (e.g., Prompt Renderer) components for fine-grained optimization.

The system integrates a component-wise content optimizer (with expanded diagnosis using both correct and incorrect cases) and a dynamic format optimizer (employing an LLM for novel format generation and UCT for selection) within a synergistic feedback loop.
paper_summary.summary: Content-Format Integrated Prompt Optimization (CFPO) introduces a methodology that jointly refines both the content and formatting of prompts for large language models. The approach achieved significant performance improvements, particularly in complex reasoning tasks like GSM8K and MATH500, consistently outperforming content-only optimization methods across diverse LLMs.

title: Beyond Prompt Content: Enhancing LLM Performance via Content-Format  Integrated Prompt Optimization

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful operators for evolutionary search, yet the design of efficient search scaffolds remains ad hoc. While promising, current LLM-in-the-loop systems lack a systematic approach to managing the evolutionary process. We identify three distinct failure modes: Context Pollution, where experiment history biases future candidate generation; Mode Collapse, where agents stagnate in local minima due to poor exploration-exploitation balance; and Weak Collaboration, where rigid crossover strategies fail to leverage parallel search trajectories effectively. We introduce Progress-Aware Consistent Evolution (PACEvolve), a framework designed to robustly govern the agent's context and search dynamics, to address these challenges. PACEvolve combines hierarchical context management (HCM) with pruning to address context pollution; momentum-based backtracking (MBB) to escape local minima; and a self-adaptive sampling policy that unifies backtracking and crossover for dynamic search coordination (CE), allowing agents to balance internal refinement with cross-trajectory collaboration. We demonstrate that PACEvolve provides a systematic path to consistent, long-horizon self-improvement, achieving state-of-the-art results on LLM-SR and KernelBench, while discovering solutions surpassing the record on Modded NanoGPT.

keyInsights: Systematic context management, including hierarchical decomposition of ideas and active pruning, is crucial for maintaining a high signal-to-noise ratio and preventing context pollution over long evolutionary horizons.
Adaptive monitoring of search progress, using metrics like relative improvement momentum, can effectively identify and mitigate mode collapse by triggering targeted interventions like backtracking.

Dynamically balancing internal refinement with external knowledge transfer in parallel searches, guided by the relative and absolute progress of each island, leads to more efficient and synergistic multi-agent evolution.
originalProblem: LLM-driven evolutionary search agents often exhibit suboptimal and unstable performance, characterized by high variance and lack of systematic management.
Context pollution occurs when search history saturates with failed hypotheses, degrading the quality of future idea generation and decision-making.
Agents frequently suffer from mode collapse, struggling to balance exploration and exploitation, which leads to stagnation in local optima rather than discovering diverse solutions.
results: PACEvolve-Single achieved state-of-the-art Log10 NMSE on the Symbolic Regression benchmark, while PACEvolve-Multi discovered three solutions with Log10 NMSE lower than -8.

On KernelBench, PACEvolve-Single outperformed existing evolutionary frameworks across all 16 GPU kernels, and PACEvolve-Multi further improved performance in 81.25% of cases, achieving speedups up to 17.38x over PyTorch.

The framework demonstrated robust automated engineering capabilities in the highly optimized Modded NanoGPT environment, discovering a series of improvements that reduced training time from 142.8s to 140.2s.
solution: Hierarchical Context Management (HCM) reforms how LLM agents store and utilize evolutionary history through bi-level pruning and persistent memory for failures.
Momentum-based Backtracking (MBB) introduces an adaptive mechanism using relative progress momentum to detect stagnation and trigger hard escapes from local minima.

Self-Adaptive Collaborative Evolution Sampling (CE) unifies backtracking and crossover into a dynamic action selection policy for multi-island setups, ensuring adaptive knowledge transfer based on progress.
paper_summary.summary: PACEvolve presents a principled framework for Large Language Model-driven evolutionary search, systematically addressing challenges like context pollution, mode collapse, and weak collaboration. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in symbolic regression, significantly optimizes GPU kernel code, and demonstrates automated research capabilities in complex engineering environments like Modded NanoGPT.

title: PACEvolve: Enabling Long-Horizon Progress-Aware Consistent Evolution

abstract:
Agentic AI systems built on large language models (LLMs) offer significant potential for automating complex workflows, from software development to customer support. However, LLM agents often underperform due to suboptimal configurations; poorly tuned prompts, tool descriptions, and parameters that typically require weeks of manual refinement. Existing optimization methods either are too complex for general use or treat components in isolation, missing critical interdependencies.
We present ARTEMIS, a no-code evolutionary optimization platform that jointly optimizes agent configurations through semantically-aware genetic operators. Given only a benchmark script and natural language goals, ARTEMIS automatically discovers configurable components, extracts performance signals from execution logs, and evolves configurations without requiring architectural modifications.
We evaluate ARTEMIS on four representative agent systems: the \emph{ALE Agent} for competitive programming on AtCoder Heuristic Contest, achieving a \textbf{$13.6\%$ improvement} in acceptance rate; the \emph{Mini-SWE Agent} for code optimization on SWE-Perf, with a statistically significant \textbf{10.1\% performance gain}; and the \emph{CrewAI Agent} for cost and mathematical reasoning on Math Odyssey, achieving a statistically significant \textbf{$36.9\%$ reduction} in the number of tokens required for evaluation. We also evaluate the \emph{MathTales-Teacher Agent} powered by a smaller open-source model (Qwen2.5-7B) on GSM8K primary-level mathematics problems, achieving a \textbf{22\% accuracy improvement} and demonstrating that ARTEMIS can optimize agents based on both commercial and local models.

keyInsights: Automated, semantically-aware optimization can discover non-obvious configurations that significantly improve agent performance beyond manual tuning.
The black-box and no-code nature of the Artemis platform democratizes advanced optimization, making it accessible to practitioners without deep expertise in evolutionary algorithms.

While effective, the computational cost of optimization remains high, and the effectiveness is greatest for agents with initial room for improvement or when optimizing secondary objectives like cost efficiency.
originalProblem: Manually tuning LLM-based agent configurations is a time-consuming, non-reproducible, and non-scalable process for expert engineers.
Optimizing agent components in isolation often leads to suboptimal performance due to complex interdependencies between prompts, tools, and parameters.
LLM agent performance cannot typically be optimized with gradient-based methods due to non-differentiable objectives and expensive evaluation costs.
results: The ALE Agent on AtCoder Heuristic Contest showed a 13.6% improvement in acceptance rate (from 66.0% to 75.0%) for prompt optimization.
The Mini-SWE Agent achieved a statistically significant 10.1% performance improvement in code optimization, with project-level gains up to 62% for specific libraries.
The CrewAI Agent on Math Odyssey demonstrated a statistically significant 36.9% decrease in average tokens used, highlighting cost efficiency optimization capabilities.
solution: Artemis uses a general-purpose evolutionary optimization platform to formalize agent optimization as a search problem over mixed-type configuration spaces.
It employs semantic genetic algorithms where LLM ensembles intelligently mutate and crossover natural language components, preserving semantic validity and coherence.
The platform operates as a black-box optimizer, requiring no architectural modifications to the agent, and uses benchmark outcomes for fitness evaluation with hierarchical evaluation strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The Artemis platform automates the optimization of LLM-based agent configurations, encompassing prompts, tool descriptions, and parameters, using semantically-aware genetic algorithms. This approach achieved performance improvements ranging from 9.3% to 36.9% across diverse tasks such as competitive programming, code optimization, and mathematical reasoning, making agent tuning more efficient and accessible.

title: Evolving Excellence: Automated Optimization of LLM-based Agents

abstract: Large Language Models excel at code generation yet struggle with complex programming tasks that demand sophisticated reasoning. To bridge this gap, traditional process supervision relies on learned reward models requiring costly training data and suffering from reward misalignment, while outcome supervision fails for complex tasks needing coordinated intermediate steps. We introduce Outcome Refining Process Supervision, which unifies process and outcome supervision by leveraging executable verification: a tree-structured search framework generates strategic alternatives, profiles execution metrics, and scores candidates via self-critique mechanisms that integrate runtime feedback with reasoning. Experiments across 5 models and 3 benchmarks show consistent gains, with 26.9% higher correctness and 42.2% improved code efficiency. The results demonstrate that ORPS enables LLMs to overcome local optima in code generation, suggesting a promising direction for combining verifiable outcomes with structured reasoning to tackle complex challenges. We open-source at: this https URL

keyInsights: Large language models' (LLMs) intrinsic reasoning and self-critique capabilities, when grounded by verifiable execution feedback, can generate high-quality process rewards without the need for separately trained Process Reward Models (PRMs).
Providing sufficient reasoning space for algorithmic exploration is often more critical for complex code generation than merely scaling model parameters.

Unifying objective execution metrics with subjective self-critique through a combined process and outcome reward score (qt = αρt + βωt) enables LLMs to explore and select superior algorithmic strategies.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step algorithmic reasoning in code generation tasks like dynamic programming.
Existing outcome supervision provides sparse pass/fail feedback, neglecting crucial intermediate reasoning steps.

Traditional process supervision, relying on trained Process Reward Models (PRMs), faces high data annotation costs, reliability issues like hallucination and reward hacking, and poor generalization for code-specific tasks.
results: The Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS) framework achieved an average Pass@1 improvement of 26.9% and an average running time reduction of 42.2% across five LLMs and three programming benchmarks.
Smaller models, when enhanced with ORPS, frequently outperformed larger models using conventional methods, demonstrating that strategic reasoning can be more critical than pure model scale.
Ablation studies confirmed that the inference-only ORPS approach significantly outperformed all trained Process Reward Models, supporting the claim that dedicated PRMs are not necessary.
solution: The Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS) framework is introduced as a novel, inference-only method that unifies process and outcome supervision for code generation.
It employs a tree-structured search, allowing for parallel exploration of diverse algorithmic strategies, where each node represents an iterative refinement of reasoning and code.

The LLM acts as both a 'programmer' to generate candidate solutions and a 'critic' to evaluate execution feedback and self-critique, generating both objective outcome rewards (from execution) and subjective process rewards (from self-critique).
paper_summary.summary: Peking University and William & Mary researchers developed Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS), a framework for large language models (LLMs) that unifies process and outcome rewards for complex code generation tasks. ORPS leverages LLMs' intrinsic reasoning and self-critique capabilities with verifiable execution feedback, achieving an average Pass@1 improvement of 26.9% and an average running time reduction of 42.2% across diverse LLMs and programming benchmarks.

title: Reasoning Through Execution: Unifying Process and Outcome Rewards for Code Generation

abstract: If autoresearch is itself a form of research, then autoresearch can be applied to research itself. We take this idea literally: we use an autoresearch loop to optimize the autoresearch loop. Every existing autoresearch system -- from Karpathy's single-track loop to AutoResearchClaw's multi-batch extension and EvoScientist's persistent memory -- was improved by a human who read the code, identified a bottleneck, and wrote new code. We ask whether an LLM can do the same, autonomously. We present Bilevel Autoresearch, a bilevel framework where an outer loop meta-optimizes the inner autoresearch loop by generating and injecting new search mechanisms as Python code at runtime. The inner loop optimizes the task; the outer loop optimizes how the inner loop searches. Both loops use the same LLM -- no stronger model is needed at the meta level. On Karpathy's GPT pretraining benchmark, the meta-autoresearch outer loop achieves a 5x improvement over the standard inner loop alone (-0.045 vs. -0.009 val_bpb), while parameter-level adjustment without mechanism change yields no reliable gain. The outer loop autonomously discovers mechanisms from combinatorial optimization, multi-armed bandits, and design of experiments -- without human specification of which domains to explore. These mechanisms succeed by breaking the inner loop's deterministic search patterns, forcing exploration of directions the LLM's priors systematically avoid. The core principle is simple: if autoresearch can meta-autoresearch itself, it can, in principle, meta-autoresearch anything with a measurable objective.

keyInsights: Autoresearch can effectively meta-autoresearch itself; an LLM can autonomously generate and inject new structural search mechanisms, leading to substantial performance gains.

Autonomous mechanism generation helps overcome implicit biases and deterministic search patterns inherent in fixed LLM-driven search loops, enabling exploration of previously overlooked optimal solutions.

The Level 2 mechanism research is the primary driver of performance improvements, while Level 1.5 strategic guidance can contribute to the robustness of the overall framework by enriching context for Level 2.
originalProblem: Existing autoresearch systems have fixed underlying search mechanisms and architectural decisions, requiring human designers to identify bottlenecks and engineer new code for improvements.

Current LLM-driven research loops can propose, execute, and evaluate task-level changes but their core search strategies are static and pre-defined, leading to repetitive proposals or parameter fixation.
Each significant advancement in autoresearch, like adding parallel search or persistent memory, has been a result of human analysis and coding, limiting the autonomy of the discovery process.
results: The full Bilevel Autoresearch framework (Group C) achieved the most significant improvement, averaging -0.045 ± 0.030 in `val_bpb`, approximately a 5x improvement over Level 1 only (-0.009 ± 0.002).
Level 2 autonomously generated diverse search mechanisms, including Tabu Search Manager, Multi-Scale Bandit Proposer, and Systematic Orthogonal Exploration, without explicit human domain guidance.

Significant performance gains in Groups C and D were largely driven by the autonomous discovery and implementation of a reduction in `TOTAL_BATCH_SIZE`, a parameter change systematically missed or blocked by Groups A and B.
solution: A three-level Bilevel Autoresearch framework is introduced, where an outer loop optimizes the search mechanism (as a program) and an inner loop optimizes task performance, all using the same LLM (DeepSeek).
Level 1 performs inner autoresearch for task optimization, proposing parameter changes and accepting them based on performance improvement within a fixed training budget.
Level 1.5 provides outer search-strategy adjustments by freezing/unfreezing parameters and injecting strategic guidance to the inner loop every 5 iterations, without altering core logic.

Level 2 performs mechanism research and code injection every 10 inner iterations, engaging in a four-round dialogue (Explore, Critique, Specify, Generate) to create and dynamically inject new Python code for search mechanisms into `runner.py`.
paper_summary.summary: Bilevel Autoresearch enables a Large Language Model to autonomously improve its own search mechanisms by generating and injecting new Python code at runtime. This meta-level optimization achieved a 5x improvement in validation bits per byte (val_bpb) for a GPT pretraining task, primarily by autonomously discovering a beneficial batch size reduction.

title: Bilevel Autoresearch: Meta-Autoresearching Itself

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable progress in scaling to access massive contexts. However, the access is via the latent and uninterpretable attention mechanisms, and LLMs fail to effective process long context, exhibiting significant performance degradation as context length increases. In this work, we study whether long-context processing can be externalized from latent attention into explicit, executable interactions, by allowing coding agents to organize text in file systems and manipulate it using its native tools. We evaluate off-the-shelf frontier coding agents as the general interface for tasks that require processing long contexts, including long-context reasoning, retrieval-augmented generation, and open-domain question answering with large-scale corpus contains up to three trillion tokens. Across multiple benchmarks, these agents outperform published state-of-the-art by 17.3% on average.
