We attribute this efficacy to two key factors: native tool proficiency, which enables agents to leverage executable code and terminal commands rather than passive semantic queries, and file system familiarity, which allows them to navigate massive text corpora as directory structures. These findings suggest that delegating long-context processing to coding agents offers an effective alternative to semantic search or context window scaling, opening new directions for long-context processing in LLMs.

keyInsights: Coding agents possess transferable software engineering skills, enabling them to process general long-context text effectively by externalizing reasoning into observable, executable steps.
Organizing documents in a hierarchical file system significantly improves agent performance by facilitating coordinate-based reading and precise data extraction.
Providing standard retrieval tools does not consistently improve performance, as agents may over-rely on imperfect retrieval, suppressing native file system exploration.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) experience "context rot," where performance declines with increasing context length despite expanded context windows, due to opaque latent attention mechanisms.
Traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines rely on fixed, shallow retrieval, hindering complex iterative or multi-hop reasoning over long texts.
ReAct-style agents often have action spaces constrained by predefined tool APIs, limiting their flexibility in open-ended long-context scenarios.
results: Off-the-shelf coding agents established state-of-the-art performance, outperforming previous best published results by an average of 17.3% across evaluated benchmarks.
The approach achieved superior results on four out of five diverse long-context benchmarks, including BrowseComp-Plus, Oolong-Synthetic, Oolong-Real, and Natural Questions.
Performance improvements were robust across context scales ranging from an average of 188,000 tokens up to three trillion tokens, demonstrated with both Codex and Claude Code.
solution: Reformulate long-context processing as a problem of file system navigation and manipulation, leveraging the agent's native proficiency with code.
Delegate these tasks to off-the-shelf frontier coding agents (e.g., Codex, Claude Code) without specialized fine-tuning.
Allow agents to autonomously execute terminal commands (e.g., `grep`, `sed`), write and run Python scripts, and create intermediate files for iterative exploration and processing.
paper_summary.summary: Off-the-shelf coding agents effectively process long textual contexts by externalizing reasoning into explicit, executable file system interactions, outperforming traditional LLMs and RAG. This method achieved state-of-the-art performance on four out of five diverse long-context benchmarks, demonstrating adaptability across various context scales.

title: Coding Agents are Effective Long-Context Processors

abstract: Prompt optimization aims to search for effective prompts that enhance the performance of large language models (LLMs). Although existing prompt optimization methods have discovered effective prompts, they often differ from sophisticated prompts carefully designed by human experts. Prompt design strategies, representing best practices for improving prompt performance, can be key to improving prompt optimization. Recently, a method termed the Autonomous Prompt Engineering Toolbox (APET) has incorporated various prompt design strategies into the prompt optimization process. In APET, the LLM is needed to implicitly select and apply the appropriate strategies because prompt design strategies can have negative effects. This implicit selection may be suboptimal due to the limited optimization capabilities of LLMs. This paper introduces Optimizing Prompts with sTrategy Selection (OPTS), which implements explicit selection mechanisms for prompt design. We propose three mechanisms, including a Thompson sampling-based approach, and integrate them into EvoPrompt, a well-known prompt optimizer. Experiments optimizing prompts for two LLMs, Llama-3-8B-Instruct and GPT-4o mini, were conducted using BIG-Bench Hard. Our results show that the selection of prompt design strategies improves the performance of EvoPrompt, and the Thompson sampling-based mechanism achieves the best overall results. Our experimental code is provided at this https URL .
title: Bandit-Based Prompt Design Strategy Selection Improves Prompt Optimizers

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in code generation tasks, yet they face significant limitations in handling complex, long-context programming challenges and demonstrating complex compositional reasoning abilities. This paper introduces a novel agentic framework for ``guided code generation'' that tries to address these limitations through a deliberately structured, fine-grained approach to code generation tasks. Our framework leverages LLMs' strengths as fuzzy searchers and approximate information retrievers while mitigating their weaknesses in long sequential reasoning and long-context understanding. Empirical evaluation using OpenAI's HumanEval benchmark with Meta's Llama 3.1 8B model (int4 precision) demonstrates a 23.79\% improvement in solution accuracy compared to direct one-shot generation. Our results indicate that structured, guided approaches to code generation can significantly enhance the practical utility of LLMs in software development while overcoming their inherent limitations in compositional reasoning and context handling.

organization_info.name: Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Code generation for complex systems is a dual process involving the retrieval of atomic components and their effective composition.
Distributing the cognitive load across specialized agents mitigates the limitations of individual Large Language Models (LLMs) in complex tasks.
Applying functional programming principles, such as modularity and pure functions, aids in the hierarchical decomposition and bottom-up composition of code.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with complex programming challenges due to limited context windows and weak compositional reasoning abilities.
Current LLMs often exhibit planning deficiencies and propagate errors, leading to poorly structured or incorrect code for substantial software engineering projects.
results: The framework achieved a Pass@1 score of 56.2% on the HumanEval benchmark using Meta's Llama 3.1 8B model.
This represents a 23.79 percentage point improvement over the baseline one-shot generation approach (45.4%).
It successfully generated complex software systems, such as a mathematical function evaluator, which some frontier models either refused or provided simpler implementations for.
solution: A multi-agent framework featuring specialized agents (Generalist, Code, Critic, Testing) for distinct roles in the code generation process.
It employs hierarchical problem decomposition to break down complex programming tasks into smaller, manageable sub-problems.
Code generation proceeds bottom-up, where atomic components are implemented, immediately tested, and then composed into more complex solutions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology developed a multi-agent framework for guided code generation that addresses the limitations of LLMs in complex programming tasks. The framework achieved a 56.2% Pass@1 score on the HumanEval benchmark using Llama 3.1 8B, marking a 23.79 percentage point improvement over baseline one-shot generation.

title: Guided Code Generation with LLMs: A Multi-Agent Framework for Complex Code Tasks

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance in general programming tasks. However, in Machine Learning Engineering (MLE) scenarios such as AutoML and Kaggle competitions, achieving high performance depends heavily on expert intervention and repeated adjustments rather than simply generating correct code. When applied directly to these tasks, LLMs often lack fine-grained domain priors, and existing MLE approaches that use linear or tree-structured searches limit knowledge transfer to adjacent hierarchical links. As a result, they cannot leverage past full trajectories or share information across branches, limiting self-evolving ability and search space diversity. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoMLGen, an LLM-based coding agent that integrates a domain knowledge base for high-quality prior guidance and Monte Carlo Graph Search (MCGS) for efficient exploration. MCGS retains the tree-guided exploration of MCTS while embedding a graph structure into the expansion stage to enable dynamic path reorganization, historical trajectory reuse, and multi-solution fusion to support both self-evolution and collaborative learning. Combined with fine-grained operator sets, this design improves stability and accelerates convergence. Evaluation on the MLE-Bench shows that AutoMLGen achieves state-of-the-art performance in numerous dimensions, such as the average medal rate and the valid submission rate, under a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Augmenting LLMs with a curated, external ML domain knowledge base effectively addresses their limitations in fine-grained domain priors, reducing "cold start" issues.

Monte Carlo Graph Search (MCGS) improves exploration efficiency and diversity by enabling flexible knowledge transfer, trajectory reuse, and multi-solution fusion across a graph-structured search space.

The integration of domain expertise with a sophisticated, graph-based search strategy allows LLM agents to perform fine-grained, end-to-end ML pipeline optimization, acting as autonomous ML engineers.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based MLE agents rely heavily on internal LLM knowledge, often lacking fine-grained domain priors for specialized ML tasks, leading to suboptimal solutions.
Current tree-structured search paradigms (e.g., MCTS) in MLE agents suffer from node isolation, restricting knowledge flow, trajectory reuse, and the fusion of insights across different search paths.
results: AutoMLGen achieved state-of-the-art performance on the MLE-Bench, with a 36.4% average medal rate and an 18.7% gold medal rate under a 12-hour budget, surpassing existing methods.
The framework demonstrated superior efficiency, reaching higher performance in half the time (12 hours) compared to many baselines that utilize 24 or 36 hours.
Ablation studies confirmed the value of the ML domain knowledge base, improving the medal rate from 40.91% to 50.00%, and MCGS components, further increasing it to 68.12%.
solution: A curated ML domain knowledge base is integrated to provide specialized, fine-grained prior guidance, categorized into model, data, and strategy levels.

Monte Carlo Graph Search (MCGS) extends MCTS by using a graph structure for expansion, enabling dynamic path reorganization, historical trajectory reuse, cross-branch referencing, and multi-solution aggregation.
A set of finer-grained operators (e.g., Draft, Debug, Improve, Fusion) supports detailed optimization and self-evolution within the MCGS framework.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Shanghai AI Lab developed AutoMLGen, an LLM-based coding agent framework that integrates a curated ML domain knowledge base and Monte Carlo Graph Search to optimize Machine Learning Engineering (MLE) pipelines. It achieved a 36.4% average medal rate on the MLE-Bench under a 12-hour budget, significantly outperforming existing methods.

title: AutoMLGen: Navigating Fine-Grained Optimization for Coding Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate exceptional performance in numerous tasks but still heavily rely on knowledge stored in their parameters. Moreover, updating this knowledge incurs high training costs. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods address this issue by integrating external knowledge. The model can answer questions it couldn't previously by retrieving knowledge relevant to the query. This approach improves performance in certain scenarios for specific tasks. However, if irrelevant texts are retrieved, it may impair model performance. In this paper, we propose Retrieval Augmented Iterative Self-Feedback (RA-ISF), a framework that iteratively decomposes tasks and processes them in three submodules to enhance the model's problem-solving capabilities. Experiments show that our method outperforms existing benchmarks, performing well on models like GPT3.5, Llama2, significantly enhancing factual reasoning capabilities and reducing hallucinations.
title: RA-ISF: Learning to Answer and Understand from Retrieval Augmentation via Iterative Self-Feedback

abstract: Discovering improved policy optimization algorithms for language models remains a costly manual process requiring repeated mechanism-level modification and validation. Unlike simple combinatorial code search, this problem requires searching over algorithmic mechanisms tightly coupled with training dynamics while reusing empirical evidence across iterations. We propose POISE, a closed-loop framework for automated discovery of policy optimization algorithms for language models. POISE maintains a structured, genealogically linked archive linking proposals, executable implementations, standardized evaluations, and natural-language reflections to support evidence-driven iteration. In mathematical reasoning experiments starting from GRPO, POISE evaluates 64 candidate algorithms and discovers improved mechanisms, including analytic-variance scaling and validity masking. The best variant improves weighted Overall from 47.8 to 52.5 (+4.6) and increases AIME25 pass@32 from 26.7% to 43.3%, demonstrating the feasibility of automated policy optimization discovery while supporting interpretable design principles.

keyInsights: LLM agents can function as "AI Scientists," autonomously proposing, implementing, and evaluating complex algorithmic changes for policy optimization in LLMs.
An acquisition function balancing Pareto-frontier strength, performance, diversity, and "descendant potential" prevents myopic search, leading to more robust algorithmic evolution.

Systematic reflection on empirical feedback allows the framework to accumulate evidence, identify recurring design principles (e.g., signal decoupling, conditional normalization), and facilitate cross-lineage knowledge transfer.
originalProblem: Discovering effective policy optimization algorithms for Large Language Models (LLMs) is a resource-intensive manual process requiring repeated proposal, implementation, and validation of algorithmic modifications.
The complex design space of policy optimization algorithms, where small changes are tightly coupled with training dynamics, makes manual exploration inefficient and challenging.

Existing manual discovery processes often lack systematic mechanisms for converting empirical feedback into reusable evidence, leading to redundant exploration and missed opportunities for cumulative learning.
results: The framework discovered VM-AV-GRPO, the strongest variant, which achieved an "Overall" score of 52.5, improving performance by 4.6 percentage points over the GRPO baseline (47.8) on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Evolutionary search revealed four recurring families of effective policy optimization mechanisms, including analytic-variance scaling and validity masking for improved signal modeling and reduced false-positive incentives.

POISE successfully demonstrated steerable search, where natural-language directives could guide the discovery of algorithms (e.g., DACE-GRPO) that reduced response length by 29.1% while also improving the "Overall" score by 3.9 percentage points.
solution: POISE (Policy Optimization through Iterative Search and Evidence) is introduced as a closed-loop, LLM-driven framework that formalizes algorithm discovery as an "Epistemic Evolutionary Search".

The framework employs three LLM-driven operators (Proposal, Verification, Reflective Analysis) to autonomously generate candidate algorithms, implement and evaluate them, and analyze empirical results.

POISE maintains a genealogically linked archive that stores algorithm proposals, executable implementations, empirical evidence (training trajectories, evaluation metrics), and natural-language reflections to inform subsequent iterative improvements.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University developed POISE, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that autonomously discovers policy optimization algorithms for language models. It identified variants like VM-AV-GRPO, which improved performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks by 4.6 percentage points over the GRPO baseline.

title: From AI Assistant to AI Scientist: Autonomous Discovery of LLM-RL Algorithms with LLM Agents

abstract: Black-box Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great power in solving various tasks and are considered general problem solvers. However, LLMs still fail in many specific tasks although understand the task instruction. In this paper, we focus on the problem of boosting the ability of black-box LLMs to solve downstream tasks. We propose ExpNote, an automated framework to help LLMs better adapt to unfamiliar tasks through reflecting and noting experiences from training data and retrieving them from external memory during testing. We evaluate ExpNote on multiple tasks and the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the performance of black-box LLMs. The data and code are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Abstracted rules are more effective than raw question-answer pairs for enabling LLMs to generalize and improve performance on new, unseen tasks.
Experiences derived from both successful (positive) and unsuccessful (negative) attempts during training contribute valuable and complementary insights for LLM learning.
An external dynamic memory and a retrieval mechanism are crucial components for empowering black-box LLMs, significantly impacting task-solving ability.
originalProblem: Black-box Large Language Models often struggle with specialized tasks requiring domain-specific knowledge or complex inductive reasoning.
Existing methods for improving LLM performance, such as parameter-efficient tuning, are not applicable to proprietary black-box models accessible only via API.
Current memory augmentation and reflection techniques either rely heavily on costly human expert annotation or fail to generalize learned insights effectively across new problem instances.
results: ExpNote consistently achieved the highest performance across four diverse datasets (CLUTRR, METS, EMOJI, LETS), demonstrating an average accuracy increase of 20.5% compared to the Chain-of-Thought method.
The framework substantially outperformed advanced baselines like TeachMe (memory-based) and Reflexion (reflection-based), even when Reflexion was given a 'cheating' advantage.
Performance on test sets showed continuous improvement as the number of training samples increased, indicating effective accumulation and utilization of learned experiences.
solution: ExpNote is an automated framework that enables black-box LLMs to reflect on their own performance and generate generalized abstract experiences.
The LLM stores these abstracted rules in a dynamic external memory using a 'NOTE' command, learning from both correct and incorrect attempts and receiving only ground-truth feedback.
During inference, ExpNote automatically retrieves up to three relevant experiences from memory using a 'RECALL' command, augmenting the LLM's prompt to guide problem-solving.
paper_summary.summary: An automated framework called ExpNote enhances the task-solving capabilities of black-box Large Language Models by enabling them to learn, abstract, and retrieve generalized experiences. This approach improved average accuracy by 20.5% over Chain-of-Thought across multiple diverse datasets, demonstrating effective adaptation without requiring human annotation.

title: ExpNote: Black-box Large Language Models are Better Task Solvers with Experience Notebook

abstract: The popularity of Large Language Models (LLMs) have unleashed a new age ofLanguage Agents for solving a diverse range of tasks. While contemporary frontier LLMs are capable enough to power reasonably good Language agents, the closed-API model makes it hard to improve in cases they perform sub-optimally. To address this, recent works have explored ways to improve their performance using techniques like self-reflection and prompt optimization. Unfortunately, techniques like self-reflection can be used only in an online setup, while contemporary prompt optimization techniques are designed and tested to work on simple tasks. To this end, we introduce MetaReflection, a novel offline reinforcement learning technique that enhances the performance of Language Agents by augmenting a semantic memory based on experiential learnings from past trials. We demonstrate the efficacy of MetaReflection by evaluating across multiple domains, including complex logical reasoning, biomedical semantic similarity, open world question answering, and vulnerability threat detection, in Infrastructure-as-Code, spanning different agent designs. MetaReflection boosts Language agents' performance by 4% to 16.82% over the raw GPT-4 baseline and performs on par with existing state-of-the-art prompt optimization techniques while requiring fewer LLM calls.
title: MetaReflection: Learning Instructions for Language Agents using Past Reflections

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in program synthesis, yet their ability to autonomously navigate neural architecture design--balancing syntactic reliability, performance, and structural novelty--remains underexplored. We address this by placing a code-oriented LLM within a closed-loop synthesis framework, analyzing its evolution over 22 supervised fine-tuning cycles. The model synthesizes PyTorch convolutional networks which are validated, evaluated via low-fidelity performance signals (single-epoch accuracy), and filtered using a MinHash-Jaccard criterion to prevent structural redundancy. High-performing, novel architectures are converted into prompt-code pairs for iterative fine-tuning via parameter-efficient LoRA adaptation, initialized from the LEMUR dataset. Across cycles, the LLM internalizes empirical architectural priors, becoming a robust generator. The valid generation rate stabilizes at 50.6 percent (peaking at 74.5 percent), while mean first-epoch accuracy rises from 28.06 percent to 50.99 percent, and the fraction of candidates exceeding 40 percent accuracy grows from 2.04 percent to 96.81 percent. Analyses confirm the model moves beyond replicating existing motifs, synthesizing 455 high-performing architectures absent from the original corpus. By grounding code synthesis in execution feedback, this work provides a scalable blueprint for transforming stochastic generators into autonomous, performance-driven neural designers, establishing that LLMs can internalize empirical, non-textual rewards to transcend their training data.

keyInsights: Large language models can internalize empirical, non-textual rewards (like low-fidelity performance signals) and structural novelty criteria through iterative self-fine-tuning, evolving beyond stochastic code generators.
The integration of a MinHash–Jaccard novelty filter is essential for sustaining architectural diversity and preventing the LLM from collapsing into redundant or near-duplicate designs.

Each component of the feedback loop (novelty filter, performance threshold, iterative fine-tuning) is crucial for the LLM's progressive improvement in generating high-quality, novel, and executable neural network designs.
originalProblem: Manual neural network architecture design is a computationally intensive and expertise-demanding bottleneck in deep learning development.
Traditional Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods incur prohibitive computational costs due to extensive training and evaluation of candidate architectures.

Existing LLM-based neural architecture generation approaches lack thorough investigation into the LLM's iterative self-improvement, treating the LLM as a static component rather than an evolving architectural prior.
results: The valid generation rate of executable PyTorch architectures steadily improved from 44.0% in Cycle 1 to a peak of 74.5% in Cycle 14, stabilizing around an overall mean of 50.6%.
The proportion of generated architectures exceeding the 40% first-epoch CIFAR-10 accuracy threshold dramatically increased from 2.04% in Cycle 1 to 96.81% in Cycle 18.

The system synthesized 455 structurally unique and high-performing architectures over 22 cycles, expanding the training corpus from 1,698 to 2,153 examples, demonstrating sustained exploration of the design space.
solution: A closed-loop, iterative "generate–evaluate–select–fine-tune" framework is employed, running over 22 cycles using a DeepSeek-Coder-7B-Instruct-v1.5 LLM.

The LLM generates candidate PyTorch architectures, which are then validated for syntactic correctness, evaluated for early-epoch CIFAR-10 classification performance, and filtered for structural novelty using MinHash–Jaccard similarity.

Architectures that are valid, high-performing (above 40% first-epoch accuracy), and structurally novel are added to a growing training corpus, which is then used to fine-tune the LLM via LoRA for the next cycle.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Würzburg developed an iterative self-fine-tuning framework that transforms a code-oriented large language model into an autonomous neural architecture designer. This system, over 22 cycles, increased the generation rate of valid PyTorch architectures from 44.0% to 74.5% and boosted the proportion of high-performing designs (above 40% first-epoch CIFAR-10 accuracy) from 2.04% to 96.81%.

title: From Memorization to Creativity: LLM as a Designer of Novel Neural-Architectures

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) generate functionally correct solutions but
often fall short in code efficiency, a critical bottleneck for real-world
deployment. In this paper, we introduce a novel test-time iterative
optimization framework to address this, employing a closed-loop system where
LLMs iteratively refine code based on empirical performance feedback from an
execution sandbox. We explore three training strategies: Supervised Fine-Tuning
(SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Group Relative Policy
Optimization (GRPO). Experiments on our Venus dataset and the APPS benchmark
show that SFT and DPO rapidly saturate in efficiency gains. In contrast, GRPO,
using reinforcement learning (RL) with execution feedback, continuously
optimizes code performance, significantly boosting both pass@1 (from 47% to
62%) and the likelihood of outperforming human submissions in efficiency (from
31% to 45%). Our work demonstrates effective test-time code efficiency
improvement and critically reveals the power of RL in teaching LLMs to truly
self-improve code efficiency.
title: Afterburner: Reinforcement Learning Facilitates Self-Improving Code  Efficiency Optimization

abstract:
Code efficiency is a fundamental aspect of software quality, yet how to harness large language models (LLMs) to optimize programs remains challenging. Prior approaches have sought for one-shot rewriting, retrieved exemplars, or prompt-based search, but they do not explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge, which limits generalization beyond individual instances.
In this paper, we present EffiSkill, a framework for code-efficiency optimization that builds a portable optimization toolbox for LLM-based agents. The key idea is to model recurring slow-to-fast transformations as reusable agent skills that capture both concrete transformation mechanisms and higher-level optimization strategies. EffiSkill adopts a two-stage design: Stage I mines Operator and Meta Skills from large-scale slow/fast program pairs to build a skill library; Stage II applies this library to unseen programs through execution-free diagnosis, skill retrieval, plan composition, and candidate generation, without runtime feedback.
Results on EffiBench-X show that EffiSkill achieves higher optimization success rates, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.69 to 12.52 percentage points across model and language settings. These findings suggest that mechanism-level skill reuse provides a useful foundation for execution-free code optimization, and that the resulting skill library can serve as a reusable resource for broader agent workflows.

keyInsights: Explicitly distilling recurring transformation patterns into a structured, portable library of "agent skills" allows LLMs to apply abstract optimization principles rather than merely imitating surface-level code patterns.
A skill-guided diagnosis, retrieval, and plan composition pipeline can effectively enable execution-free code optimization, addressing practical limitations of online feedback.
Diversified plan exploration and the accurate retrieval of relevant skills are crucial components for discovering high-quality, functionally correct, and measurably faster code.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based code optimization methods often operate at an instance-specific level, struggling with multi-step reasoning and failing to explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge.
Prior data-driven approaches frequently rely on iterative execution feedback during the optimization process, which is often unavailable or costly in real-world software engineering scenarios.
Traditional code optimization relies on manually designed rules and expert-driven techniques, which typically offer limited coverage and require substantial human effort.
results: EffiSkill consistently achieved higher optimization success rates, outperforming the strongest baselines by up to +12.52 percentage points for OPT@8 on EffiBench-X across Python and C++ using GPT-5-mini and Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct.

Ablation studies confirmed that each component of EffiSkill's inference stage (skill retrieval, relevant skill selection, and multi-plan composition) significantly contributed to its overall performance.

The learned skill library consists of 29 compact and diverse Operator Skills, categorized into five semantic families, demonstrating a core set of frequently applicable skills that enable the composition of effective optimization "recipes."
solution: EffiSkill employs a two-stage design: offline skill mining, where recurrent slow-to-fast program transformations are distilled into structured "agent skills," and skill-guided optimization during inference.

It formalizes two types of agent skills: "Operator Skills" that encode concrete transformation mechanisms (e.g., algorithm replacement), and "Meta Skills" that capture higher-level control logic for orchestrating optimization.

During inference, the framework utilizes Meta Skills for diagnosis, retrieves relevant Operator Skills, composes diverse optimization plans, and generates optimized code candidates without execution feedback.
paper_summary.summary: EffiSkill, a framework developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, University of California, Berkeley, and other institutions, enables Large Language Models to optimize code efficiency by distilling and reusing optimization knowledge as agent skills. It achieved up to +12.52 percentage points higher OPT@8 success rates compared to state-of-the-art baselines on the EffiBench-X benchmark for Python and C++, enabling execution-free code improvement.

title: EffiSkill: Agent Skill Based Automated Code Efficiency Optimization

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) has exhibited strong reasoning ability in text-based contexts across various domains, yet the limitation of context window poses challenges for the model on long-range inference tasks and necessitates a memory storage system. While many current storage approaches have been proposed with episodic notes and graph representations of memory, retrieval methods still primarily rely on predefined workflows or static similarity top-k over embeddings. To address this inflexibility, we introduced a novel tool-augmented autonomous memory retrieval framework (TA-Mem), which contains: (1) a memory extraction LLM agent which is prompted to adaptively chuck an input into sub-context based on semantic correlation, and extract information into structured notes, (2) a multi-indexed memory database designed for different types of query methods including both key-based lookup and similarity-based retrieval, (3) a tool-augmented memory retrieval agent which explores the memory autonomously by selecting appropriate tools provided by the database based on the user input, and decides whether to proceed to the next iteration or finalizing the response after reasoning on the fetched memories. The TA-Mem is evaluated on the LoCoMo dataset, achieving significant performance improvements over existing baseline approaches. In addition, an analysis of tool use across different question types also demonstrates the adaptivity of the proposed method.

keyInsights: Empowering LLMs with autonomous tool selection for memory retrieval enables dynamic adaptation to specific query types, moving beyond static, one-size-fits-all approaches.
Structuring memory into rich, multi-indexed notes (e.g., facts, events, persons, tags) provides versatile access points, allowing for more targeted and precise information retrieval.
Integrating an iterative agentic loop with a memory cache optimizes token usage by focusing on relevant context and preventing redundant fetching, even with multiple LLM interactions.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with long-range inference due to finite context windows, limiting their ability to recall information from extensive past interactions.
Existing memory retrieval methods for LLMs are often static and inflexible, relying on predefined top-k similarity searches or rigid workflows, which can lead to irrelevant or missed context.
Suboptimal context provision from static retrieval results in information redundancy, increased token usage, and potentially compromised LLM reasoning quality.
results: TA-Mem achieved the highest F1 score (55.95) and BLEU-1 score (51.47) on temporal questions, and the highest BLEU-1 scores for multi-hop and open-domain questions on the LoCoMo dataset.
The framework maintained competitive token efficiency with an average of 3755 tokens per question, indicating successful concentration of relevant information despite the iterative agentic loop.

Analysis of tool usage showed adaptive strategies, with event queries predominantly used for temporal questions and fact queries for open-domain questions, validating the system's flexible memory exploration.
solution: An Episodic Memory Constructor uses an LLM agent to semantically chunk conversation history and extract structured memory notes, including summaries, keywords, persons, facts, events, and temporal references, in a single interaction.
A Multi-Indexed Database stores these memory pages, supporting diverse query methods via various indices (e.g., person names, tags, embeddings of events/facts) and exposing specialized query tools.

A Memory Retrieval Agent, an iterative LLM, autonomously selects the most appropriate query tools, generates parameters, fetches context, and decides to continue searching or finalize the answer, employing a memory cache for efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: TA-Mem is a tool-augmented framework that empowers Large Language Models to autonomously retrieve information from memory in long-term conversational QA. It achieves superior performance, particularly on temporal and multi-hop questions, while maintaining token efficiency by adapting retrieval strategies to query types.

title: TA-Mem: Tool-Augmented Autonomous Memory Retrieval for LLM in Long-Term Conversational QA

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to learn in-context, allowing them to perform various tasks based on a few input-output examples. However, the effectiveness of in-context learning is heavily reliant on the quality of the selected examples. In this paper, we propose a novel framework to iteratively train dense retrievers that can identify high-quality in-context examples for LLMs. Our framework initially trains a reward model based on LLM feedback to evaluate the quality of candidate examples, followed by knowledge distillation to train a bi-encoder based dense retriever. Our experiments on a suite of $30$ tasks demonstrate that our framework significantly enhances in-context learning performance. Furthermore, we show the generalization ability of our framework to unseen tasks during training. An in-depth analysis reveals that our model improves performance by retrieving examples with similar patterns, and the gains are consistent across LLMs of varying sizes. The code and data are available at this https URL .
title: Learning to Retrieve In-Context Examples for Large Language Models

abstract: Current large language model (LLM) applications often employ multi-component prompts, comprising both system and user prompts, to guide model behaviors. While recent advancements have demonstrated the efficacy of automatically optimizing either the system or user prompt to boost performance, such unilateral approaches often yield suboptimal outcomes due to the interdependent nature of these components. In this work, we introduce P3, a novel self-improvement framework that concurrently optimizes both system and user prompts through an iterative process. The offline optimized prompts are further leveraged to promote online prompting by performing query-dependent prompt optimization. Extensive experiments on general tasks (e.g., Arena-hard and Alpaca-eval) and reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K and GPQA) demonstrate that P3 achieves superior performance in the realm of automatic prompt optimization. Our results highlight the effectiveness of a holistic optimization strategy in enhancing LLM performance across diverse domains.

keyInsights: Jointly optimizing system prompts and user prompt complements overcomes the 'affinity issue' by ensuring system prompts align with dynamic user queries, leading to improved performance.
Generating diverse user prompt complements through iterative refinement, rather than fixed strategies, enhances the exploration of the solution space and yields more robust results.

Leveraging in-context learning (P3-ICL) for online prompt adjustment offers comparable or superior performance to fine-tuning smaller models while drastically reducing computational overhead and inference latency.
originalProblem: Existing online prompt optimization methods often overlook the interdependent nature of system and user prompts, leading to a 'affinity issue' where static system prompts limit optimization.
Current prompt optimization techniques frequently employ fixed rephrasing or extension strategies, which restrict the exploration of the vast solution space for effective prompts ('diversity issue').
Many online prompt optimization approaches incur significant inference cost and latency due to the need for fine-tuning and serially running additional smaller LLMs, hindering real-time application.
results: P3 consistently outperforms existing methods like BPO and PAS across general QA (e.g., achieving 57.16% on Alpaca-Eval 2.0 vs. 47.52% for PAS) and reasoning tasks (e.g., improving GPT-3.5-turbo on GSM8k from 72.9% to 84.8%).
P3's joint optimization of system and user prompts yields better results than optimizing user prompts alone, particularly for prompt-sensitive LLMs like GPT-3.5-turbo and Qwen2.

P3-ICL, the in-context learning variant, reduces memory occupancy by 72% (from 18000 MiB to 5000 MiB) and extra latency by 87% (from 530 ms to 70 ms) compared to fine-tuning methods, while maintaining competitive performance.
solution: P3 introduces a two-stage framework: offline joint optimization of system and user prompts, followed by efficient online optimization.

The offline stage iteratively refines user prompt complements and system prompts, using an LLM-as-optimizer and an LLM-as-judge, to generate diverse high-quality (user prompt, complement) pairs and an optimized system prompt.

For online optimization, P3 offers two methods: fine-tuning a small LLM on the collected data, or a more efficient P3-ICL approach that retrieves relevant demonstrations for in-context learning by the primary LLM.
paper_summary.summary: P3 (Prompts Promote Prompting), a framework developed by Renmin University of China and Huawei Poisson Lab, enhances LLM performance by concurrently optimizing both system and user prompts. The approach generates diverse user prompt complements and an adaptive system prompt offline, enabling efficient, real-time in-context learning (P3-ICL) for online inference, which reduces computational overhead while outperforming prior methods on various benchmarks.

title: P3: Prompts Promote Prompting

abstract: We present Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Code Synthesis (ARCS), a system that improves LLM-based code generation without fine-tuning. ARCS operates through a budgeted synthesize-execute-repair loop over a frozen model: it retrieves relevant code context before generation, proposes candidates, executes them against tests, and repairs based on execution feedback. This retrieval-before-generation design reduces hallucination and accelerates convergence. We formalize ARCS as a state-action process with provable guarantees on termination, monotonic improvement, and bounded cost. A tiered controller (Small/Medium/Large) trades latency for accuracy predictably. On HumanEval, ARCS achieves up to 87.2% pass@1 with Llama-3.1-405B, surpassing CodeAgent (82.3%) while using simpler control than tree-search methods. On TransCoder, it achieves >= 90% accuracy on most translation pairs. On a LANL scientific corpus, it improves CodeBLEU by +0.115 over baseline RAG. ARCS provides a practical, reproducible approach to reliable code synthesis using existing LLM checkpoints.
title: ARCS: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Code Synthesis with Iterative Refinement

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in learning algorithms, evaluations, and optimization tasks. Recent studies have shown that using LLM-based optimizers to automatically optimize model prompts, demonstrations, predictions themselves, or other components can significantly enhance the performance of AI systems, as demonstrated by frameworks such as DSPy and TextGrad. However, optimizers built on language models themselves are usually designed by humans with manual design choices; optimizers themselves are not optimized. Moreover, these optimizers are general purpose by design, to be useful to a broad audience, and are not tailored for specific tasks. To address these challenges, we propose metaTextGrad, which focuses on designing a meta-optimizer to further enhance existing optimizers and align them to be good optimizers for a given task. Our approach consists of two key components: a meta prompt optimizer and a meta structure optimizer. The combination of these two significantly improves performance across multiple benchmarks, achieving an average absolute performance improvement of up to 6% compared to the best baseline.

keyInsights: Optimizing the optimizers themselves leads to LLM programs that are more aligned with specific tasks and handle intricate details more effectively, often achieving significant improvements within the first 1-2 steps.
Meta-optimization can enable smaller, more cost-effective LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o-mini) to achieve performance comparable to or surpassing larger, more expensive models in zero-shot settings.
The learned optimization strategies are transferable across different language models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini to Claude 3 Haiku) and datasets, suggesting robustness and generalizability.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based optimizers are typically human-designed, fixed in their strategies, and general-purpose, leading to suboptimal performance for diverse tasks.
These optimizers often use generic prompts not tailored to specific tasks, despite LLMs' sensitivity to instruction wording.
General-purpose optimizers struggle to learn effectively from sparse or noisy feedback, potentially incorporating detrimental suggestions into the LLM programs they optimize.
results: `metaTextGrad` achieved an average absolute performance improvement of up to 6% compared to the best baseline, with up to an 11% absolute improvement on specific tasks like BBH Dyck Languages.

It demonstrated cost-effectiveness; for instance, on BBH Dyck Languages, `metaTextGrad` with GPT-4o-mini achieved 37% accuracy for $0.44, outperforming Zero-shot CoT with GPT-4o (18% accuracy for $0.52).
On challenging benchmarks, `metaTextGrad` achieved 77% test accuracy with Qwen models on BBH Dyck Languages and a 40% success rate on ARC-AGI, surpassing all baselines.
solution: The `metaTextGrad` framework introduces a meta-optimizer to automatically refine and combine existing LLM optimizers, treating LLM calls as black-box operations.
A Meta Prompt Optimizer refines the prompts of individual LLM optimizers to improve their task alignment.
A Meta Structure Optimizer automatically determines the optimal combination and sequence of different optimization modules, forming a composite optimizer.
paper_summary.summary: metaTextGrad, a framework developed by researchers at Tsinghua and Stanford Universities, automates the optimization of large language model (LLM) optimizers themselves, enhancing their ability to create effective LLM programs for specific tasks. It achieved an average absolute performance improvement of up to 6% across various benchmarks and enabled smaller LLMs to outperform larger models in zero-shot settings while being more cost-effective.

title: metaTextGrad: Automatically optimizing language model optimizers

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are widely adopted for assisting in software development tasks, yet their performance evaluations have narrowly focused on the functional correctness of generated code. Human programmers, however, require LLM-generated code to be not only correct but also optimally efficient. We propose PerfCodeGen, a training-free framework that enhances the performance of LLM-generated code by incorporating feedback based on runtime during test case execution into the self-refinement iterations. With PerfCodeGen, we achieve speedups for a significantly higher proportion of problems compared to using the base LLM with sophisticated prompting techniques. Applied to open language models like Phi-3-mini, PerfCodeGen achieves runtime efficiency comparable to prompting powerful closed models like GPT-4. We achieve state-of-the-art runtime efficiency on benchmarks such as HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS, frequently surpassing the ground truth reference solutions with PerfCodeGen using GPT-3.5 and GPT-4. Additionally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in enhancing code quality across a range of open LLMs of varying sizes including Phi-3-mini, Llama 3 8B, Mixtral 8x7B, Command R, and Llama 3 70B.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

title: PerfCodeGen: Improving Performance of LLM Generated Code with Execution Feedback

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have transformed software development through code generation capabilities, yet their effectiveness for high-performance computing (HPC) remains limited. HPC code requires specialized optimizations for parallelism, memory efficiency, and architecture-specific considerations that general-purpose LLMs often overlook. We present MARCO (Multi-Agent Reactive Code Optimizer), a novel framework that enhances LLM-generated code for HPC through a specialized multi-agent architecture. MARCO employs separate agents for code generation and performance evaluation, connected by a feedback loop that progressively refines optimizations. A key innovation is MARCO's web-search component that retrieves real-time optimization techniques from recent conference proceedings and research publications, bridging the knowledge gap in pre-trained LLMs. Our extensive evaluation on the LeetCode 75 problem set demonstrates that MARCO achieves a 14.6\% average runtime reduction compared to Claude 3.5 Sonnet alone, while the integration of the web-search component yields a 30.9\% performance improvement over the base MARCO system. These results highlight the potential of multi-agent systems to address the specialized requirements of high-performance code generation, offering a cost-effective alternative to domain-specific model fine-tuning.

keyInsights: Dynamically integrating real-time knowledge via a web-search component is critical for LLMs to generate state-of-the-art HPC optimizations, overcoming static training data limitations.
An iterative, multi-agent feedback loop allows for progressive code refinement, mimicking human expert problem-solving and leading to more robust and performant code than single-pass generation.

Specialized agent roles for optimization, knowledge retrieval, and performance evaluation enable a targeted approach to address complex HPC challenges, making the system adaptable and cost-effective compared to domain-specific model fine-tuning.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to generate optimal code for High-Performance Computing (HPC) due to a lack of specialized knowledge in parallelism, memory efficiency, and architecture-specific optimizations.
Pre-trained LLMs have knowledge cutoffs, making them unaware of the latest advancements, architectures, and optimization strategies constantly emerging in the HPC domain.
Manual HPC code optimization is complex and labor-intensive, requiring deep expert knowledge, while fine-tuning LLMs for this domain is expensive and resource-intensive.
results: MARCO achieved an average runtime reduction of 14.6% across the LeetCode 75 problem set compared to Claude 3.5 Sonnet, with a 51.9% average runtime improvement on hard problems.
The web-search component alone contributed a 30.9% performance improvement over MARCO without web search, highlighting the value of dynamic knowledge integration.
Memory usage was modestly improved by an average of 1.2% overall, with up to 10.8% reduction on hard problems, generally maintaining or improving memory efficiency while optimizing for speed.
solution: MARCO employs a multi-agent architecture comprising a Code Optimizer Agent, a Web-Search Engine, and a Performance Evaluator Agent, operating in a coordinated iterative feedback loop.
The Web-Search Engine dynamically retrieves current HPC optimization techniques from scholarly sources (e.g., acm.org, arxiv.org) to overcome LLM knowledge cutoffs.
The Performance Evaluator Agent benchmarks optimized code for execution time, memory usage, and complexity, providing quantitative feedback to the Code Optimizer Agent for iterative refinement.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Virginia Tech developed MARCO, a multi-agent AI framework that optimizes Large Language Model (LLM)-generated code for High-Performance Computing (HPC) by integrating real-time web-search for dynamic knowledge acquisition. This system achieved an average runtime reduction of 14.6% on the LeetCode 75 problem set and a 51.9% improvement on hard problems, outperforming baseline LLMs.

title: MARCO: Multi-Agent Code Optimization with Real-Time Knowledge Integration for High-Performance Computing

abstract: Large language models are strong sequence predictors, yet standard inference relies on immutable context histories. After making an error at generation step t, the model lacks an updatable memory mechanism that improves predictions for step t+1. We propose LLM-as-RNN, an inference-only framework that turns a frozen LLM into a recurrent predictor by representing its hidden state as natural-language memory. This state, implemented as a structured system-prompt summary, is updated at each timestep via feedback-driven text rewrites, enabling learning without parameter updates. Under a fixed token budget, LLM-as-RNN corrects errors and retains task-relevant patterns, effectively performing online learning through language. We evaluate the method on three sequential benchmarks in healthcare, meteorology, and finance across Llama, Gemma, and GPT model families. LLM-as-RNN significantly outperforms zero-shot, full-history, and MemPrompt baselines, improving predictive accuracy by 6.5% on average, while producing interpretable, human-readable learning traces absent in standard context accumulation.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Explicitly representing an LLM's internal "hidden state" as a mutable, natural-language memory enables online adaptation and error correction during inference without parameter updates.

Active, feedback-driven curation and rewriting of context through language is more effective for long-horizon tasks than simply accumulating raw or summarized history, mitigating issues like attention dilution.
The framework provides human-readable learning traces of the model's evolving understanding, past errors, and refined strategies, enhancing interpretability and trust for critical applications.
originalProblem: Standard LLM inference is "stateless," relying on immutable, append-only context histories, which leads to error propagation, attention dilution, and an inability to update internal beliefs over long sequences.

Existing context management methods like Full History Concatenation (FHC) and summarization-based prompts (MemPrompt) struggle with scalability and lack mechanisms for actively correcting past errors or updating belief states.

There is a need for online adaptation in sequential tasks, such as longitudinal clinical prediction, where models must continuously learn and adjust to evolving data distributions without costly retraining.
results: Achieved the strongest overall performance across most benchmarks, with an average predictive accuracy improvement of 6.5% compared to baselines.
Demonstrated significant gains in dynamic domains, including a 12.6% absolute improvement in Acc@1 on MIMIC-IV clinical diagnosis and a 6.6% MSE reduction in S&P 500 financial forecasting.

Exhibited performance improvement over sequential timesteps and a "self-healing" behavior, correcting prior errors in 54.8% of cases after incorporating feedback, and enabled a smaller 3B Llama model to outperform a 70B Llama model using Full History Concatenation.
solution: The LLM-as-RNN framework re-conceptualizes sequential prediction as a recurrent process within a frozen LLM by maintaining and updating a natural-language memory state.
It employs a three-step recurrent inference mechanism at each timestep: Contextualization (forming current input), Reflection (generating natural-language feedback on predictions), and Memory Update.
The Memory Update step leverages the LLM's language capabilities to apply critiques and rewrite incorrect beliefs within the memory, ensuring a fixed token budget and error-driven self-correction.
paper_summary.summary: The LLM-as-RNN framework introduces a recurrent language model that enables frozen large language models (LLMs) to perform inference-time adaptation and error correction by maintaining and updating a natural-language memory state. This approach improves average predictive accuracy by 6.5% across diverse sequential tasks and facilitates self-healing behavior to rectify past mistakes, particularly benefiting dynamic domains like clinical prediction.

title: LLM-as-RNN: A Recurrent Language Model for Memory Updates and Sequence Prediction

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities in general coding tasks but encounter two key challenges when optimizing code: (i) the complexity of writing optimized code (such as performant CUDA kernels and competition-level CPU code) requires expertise in systems, algorithms and specific languages and (ii) requires interpretation of performance metrics like timing and device utilization beyond binary correctness. In this work, we explore inference-time search algorithms that guide the LLM to discover better solutions through iterative refinement based on execution feedback. Our approach, called MaxCode unifies existing search methods under a max-reward reinforcement learning framework, making the observation and action-value functions modular for modification. To enhance the observation space, we integrate a natural language critique model that converts raw execution feedback into diagnostic insights about errors and performance bottlenecks, and the best-discounted reward seen so far. Together, these provide richer input to the code proposal function. To improve exploration during search, we train a generative reward-to-go model using action values from rollouts to rerank potential solutions. Testing on the KernelBench (CUDA) and PIE (C++) optimization benchmarks shows that MaxCode improves optimized code performance compared to baselines, achieving 20.3% and 10.1% relative improvements in absolute speedup value and relative speedup ranking, respectively.

keyInsights: Formalizing code optimization as a max-reward RL problem provides a more direct objective for achieving peak performance compared to maximizing cumulative rewards.
Supplying LLMs with human-interpretable natural language critiques significantly improves their capacity to diagnose and correct performance bottlenecks in code.
Explicitly embedding the best performance achieved into the LLM's observation space during search drives a more targeted and effective optimization process.
Combining full trajectory information, natural language critiques, and best-performance tracking creates a synergistic effect that enhances the overall optimization outcome.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to produce highly optimized code due to the deep expertise required in systems, algorithms, and specific hardware architectures.
Existing LLM-based optimization methods poorly interpret raw, complex execution feedback (e.g., timing, device utilization) into actionable diagnostic insights.
Iterative code optimization often relies on extensive trial-and-error without principled guidance, making the search for optimal solutions inefficient.
results: MAXCODE consistently improved performance of baseline methods (Effi-Learner, CUDA-LLM), yielding relative speedup improvements of 27.3% on KernelBench Level 1, 11.0% on KernelBench Level 2, and 22.5% on PIE when integrated with CUDA-LLM.
Ablation studies confirmed that the combination of trajectory information, natural language critiques, and explicit best-performance tracking was crucial for achieving the highest speedup gains.
The framework demonstrated superior inference-time scaling, enabling existing search methods to achieve higher median maximum speedups more quickly for a given computational budget.

The learned generative reward-to-go model showed mixed results, indicating potential for search guidance but also highlighting challenges in accurate maximum speedup estimation and handling distribution shifts.
solution: The framework recasts automated code optimization as a max-reward reinforcement learning problem, focusing on finding the single best solution within a trajectory.
It augments the LLM's observation space with a dedicated natural language critique model that translates raw execution feedback into diagnostic insights and concrete refinement suggestions.
An auxiliary variable (u) is introduced to explicitly track the best-discounted reward achieved so far, guiding the LLM's search towards superior performance.
A generative reward-to-go model is developed to predict potential future performance of candidate code, aiming to improve search efficiency by prioritizing promising solutions.
paper_summary.summary: MAXCODE introduces a max-reward reinforcement learning framework for automated code optimization, enhancing Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to generate performant code. The framework provides LLMs with enriched observation spaces, including natural language critiques and explicit tracking of the best performance achieved, resulting in significant speedup improvements for CUDA kernels and competitive C++ problems.

title: MaxCode: A Max-Reward Reinforcement Learning Framework for Automated Code Optimization

abstract: Large language models are transforming systems research by automating the discovery of performance-critical algorithms for computer systems. Despite plausible codes generated by LLMs, producing solutions that meet the stringent correctness and performance requirements of systems demands iterative optimization. Test-time reinforcement learning offers high search efficiency but requires parameter updates infeasible under API-only access, while existing training-free evolutionary methods suffer from inefficient context utilization and undirected search. We introduce ContextEvolve, a multi-agent framework that achieves RL-level search efficiency under strict parameter-blind constraints by decomposing optimization context into three orthogonal dimensions: a Summarizer Agent condenses semantic state via code-to-language abstraction, a Navigator Agent distills optimization direction from trajectory analysis, and a Sampler Agent curates experience distribution through prioritized exemplar retrieval. This orchestration forms a functional isomorphism with RL-mapping to state representation, policy gradient, and experience replay-enabling principled optimization in a textual latent space. On the ADRS benchmark, ContextEvolve outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 33.3% while reducing token consumption by 29.0%. Codes for our work are released at this https URL

keyInsights: Decomposing and compressing the optimization context into distinct, semantically rich dimensions via specialized agents effectively mitigates context window limitations and information bloat in LLM-driven iterative processes.
Achieving RL-level search efficiency in a training-free, parameter-blind setting is possible by functionally emulating RL mechanisms through multi-agent interactions in a textual latent space.

Directing LLM-based code evolution benefits more from abstract, high-level guidance and semantically informative experience sampling (even from lower-scoring or failed attempts) than from overly specific instructions or solely high-performing examples.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based iterative optimization methods face limitations due to the computational cost of test-time reinforcement learning and the inability to update LLM parameters with API-only access.
Training-free evolutionary approaches suffer from low search efficiency caused by inefficient context utilization (information bloat) and undirected exploration within the LLM's context window.
The strict correctness and performance demands of systems code require robust optimization techniques that consistently surpass raw LLM outputs and human-engineered baselines.
results: ContextEvolve consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines across five ADRS benchmarks, yielding an average score improvement of 33.3% and updating the best-so-far solution 83.3% more frequently.

The framework achieved an average reduction in token consumption by 17.3% compared to existing evolutionary methods, by curating fewer, high-value exemplars and increasing information density per token.

In the Load Balancing task, ContextEvolve discovered algorithmic breakthroughs, such as a 'largest remainder proportional apportionment strategy,' boosting balancedness by over 36% compared to baselines.
solution: ContextEvolve introduces a multi-agent framework comprising a Summarizer, Navigator, and Sampler, which collectively compress raw interaction logs into a semantically rich optimization context.

The framework establishes a functional isomorphism with core Reinforcement Learning (RL) concepts, including state representation, policy gradient, and experience replay, operating entirely within a textual latent space.

Specialized agents manage orthogonal dimensions of the optimization context: the Summarizer condenses semantic states, the Navigator distills optimization directions, and the Sampler curates informative few-shot exemplars for the Generator LLM.
paper_summary.summary: ContextEvolve presents a multi-agent framework designed for efficient iterative optimization of systems code using Large Language Models under API-only access constraints. The framework achieves an average score improvement of 33.3% over state-of-the-art baselines and reduces token consumption by 17.3% while frequently updating the best-so-far solution 83.3% more often.

title: ContextEvolve: Multi-Agent Context Compression for Systems Code Optimization

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have recently achieved remarkable results in
complex multi-step tasks, such as mathematical reasoning and agentic software
engineering. However, they often struggle to maintain consistent performance
across multiple solution attempts. One effective approach to narrow the gap
between average-case and best-case performance is guided test-time search,
which explores multiple solution paths to identify the most promising one.
Unfortunately, effective search techniques (e.g. MCTS) are often unsuitable for
non-serializable RL environments, such as Docker containers, where intermediate
environment states cannot be easily saved and restored. We investigate two
complementary search strategies applicable to such environments: 1-step
lookahead and trajectory selection, both guided by a learned action-value
function estimator. On the SWE-bench Verified benchmark, a key testbed for
agentic software engineering, we find these methods to double the average
success rate of a fine-tuned Qwen-72B model, achieving 40.8%, the new
state-of-the-art for open-weights models. Additionally, we show that these
techniques are transferable to more advanced closed models, yielding similar
improvements with GPT-4o.
title: Guided Search Strategies in Non-Serializable Environments with  Applications to Software Engineering Agents

abstract: Inference-time techniques are emerging as highly effective tools to enhance large language model (LLM) capabilities. However, best practices for developing systems that combine these techniques remain underdeveloped due to our limited understanding of the utility of individual inference-time techniques and the interactions between them. Additionally, efficiently and automatically searching the space of model choices, inference-time techniques, and their compositions is challenging due to the large design space. To address these challenges, we introduce Archon, a modular framework for selecting, combining, and stacking layers of inference-time techniques to construct optimized LLM systems for target benchmarks. Rather than relying on a single LLM called once, we leverage a diverse set of LLMs and inference-time techniques, creating LLM systems greater than the sum of their parts. Archon defines an extensible design space, encompassing techniques such as generation ensembling, repeated sampling, ranking, fusion, critiquing, verification, and unit testing. It transforms the problem of building LLM systems into a hyperparameter optimization objective. Given the available LLMs, inference-time techniques, and compute budget, Archon utilizes hyperparameter search techniques to discover optimized architectures for target benchmark(s). We evaluate Archon architectures across a range of instruction-following, reasoning, and coding benchmarks, including MT-Bench, Arena-Hard-Auto, AlpacaEval 2.0, MixEval, MixEval Hard, MATH, and CodeContests. Archon architectures outperform frontier models, such as GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, on these benchmarks, achieving an average accuracy increase of 15.1 percentage points by using all available LLMs. We make our code and datasets available publicly on Github: this https URL.
title: Archon: An Architecture Search Framework for Inference-Time Techniques

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved notable performance in code synthesis; however, data-aware augmentation remains a limiting factor, handled via heuristic design or brute-force approaches. We introduce a performance-aware, closed-loop solution in the NNGPT ecosystem of projects that enables LLMs to autonomously engineer optimal transformations by internalizing empirical performance cues. We fine-tune LLMs with Low-Rank Adaptation on a novel repository of more than 6,000 empirically evaluated PyTorch augmentation functions, each annotated solely by downstream model accuracy. Training uses pairwise performance ordering (better-worse transformations), enabling alignment through empirical feedback without reinforcement learning, reward models, or symbolic objectives. This reduces the need for exhaustive search, achieving up to 600x times fewer evaluated candidates than brute-force discovery while maintaining competitive peak accuracy and shifting generation from random synthesis to task-aligned design. Ablation studies show that structured Chain-of-Thought prompting introduces syntactic noise and degrades performance, whereas direct prompting ensures stable optimization in performance-critical code tasks. Qualitative and quantitative analyses demonstrate that the model internalizes semantic performance cues rather than memorizing syntax. These results show that LLMs can exhibit task-level reasoning through non-textual feedback loops, bypassing explicit symbolic rewards.
title: From Brute Force to Semantic Insight: Performance-Guided Data Transformation Design with LLMs

abstract: We introduce Output-Space Search (OS-Search), which turns LLM generation into endpoint search. An outer loop selects a target z* in a frozen encoder-defined 3D output space Z, and a retrieval-grounded policy trained with sequence-level RL generates outputs whose coordinates land near z* under standard autoregressive decoding. This enables parallel sweeps and black-box optimization in Z without path-dependent token/program search. On stories, sweeping Z (text) yields 3.1x higher LLM-scored diversity than prompt-chaining. On code, Bayesian optimization over Z (code) improves an objective withheld from the controller under matched inference budgets while preserving validity.
title: Output-Space Search: Targeting LLM Generations in a Frozen Encoder-Defined Output Space

abstract: Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been applied to scientific equation discovery, leveraging their embedded scientific knowledge for hypothesis generation. However, current methods typically confine LLMs to the role of an equation proposer within search algorithms like genetic programming. In this paper, we present SR-Scientist, a framework that elevates the LLM from a simple equation proposer to an autonomous AI scientist that writes code to analyze data, implements the equation as code, submits it for evaluation, and optimizes the equation based on experimental feedback. Specifically, we wrap the code interpreter into a set of tools for data analysis and equation evaluation. The agent is instructed to optimize the equation by utilizing these tools over a long horizon with minimal human-defined pipelines. Empirical results show that SR-Scientist outperforms baseline methods by an absolute margin of 6% to 35% on datasets covering four science disciplines. Additionally, we demonstrate our method's robustness to noise, the generalization of the discovered equations to out-of-domain data, and their symbolic accuracy. Furthermore, we develop an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework to enhance the agent's capabilities.
title: SR-Scientist: Scientific Equation Discovery With Agentic AI

abstract: Existing memory systems enable Large Language Models (LLMs) to support long-horizon human-LLM interactions by persisting historical interactions beyond limited context windows. However, while recent approaches have succeeded in constructing effective memories, they often disrupt the inherent logical and temporal relationships within interaction sessions, resulting in fragmented memory units and degraded reasoning performance. In this paper, we propose MetaMem, a novel framework that augments memory systems with a self-evolving meta-memory, aiming to teach LLMs how to effectively utilize memorized knowledge. During meta-memory optimization, MetaMem iteratively distills transferable knowledge utilization experiences across different tasks by self-reflecting on reasoning processes and performing actions to update the current meta-memory state. The accumulated meta-memory units serve as explicit knowledge utilization experiences, guiding the LLM to systematically identify and integrate critical evidence from scattered memory fragments. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of MetaMem, which significantly outperforms strong baselines by over 3.6%. All codes and datasets are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: MetaMem: Evolving Meta-Memory for Knowledge Utilization through Self-Reflective Symbolic Optimization

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated superior results across a wide range of tasks, and Retrieval-augmented Generation (RAG) is an effective way to enhance the performance by locating relevant information and placing it into the context window of the LLM. However, the relationship between retrievers and LLMs in a RAG is still under-investigated. Most existing work treats the retriever and the LLM as independent components and leaves a gap between retrieving human-"friendly" information and assembling a LLM-"friendly" context. In this work, we examine a novel bridge mechanism. We validate the ranking and selection assumptions of retrievers in the context of RAG and propose a framework that chains together supervised and reinforcement learning to train a bridge model that optimizes the connection between the retriever and the LLM. Empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in both question-answering and personalized generation tasks.
title: Bridging the Preference Gap between Retrievers and LLMs

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit world knowledge and inference capabilities, making them powerful tools for various applications. This paper proposes a feedback loop mechanism that leverages these capabilities to tune Evolution Strategies (ES) parameters effectively. The mechanism involves a structured process of providing programming instructions, executing the corresponding code, and conducting thorough analysis. This process is specifically designed for the optimization of ES parameters. The method operates through an iterative cycle, ensuring continuous refinement of the ES parameters. First, LLMs process the instructions to generate or modify the code. The code is then executed, and the results are meticulously logged. Subsequent analysis of these results provides insights that drive further improvements. An experiment on tuning the learning rates of ES using the LLaMA3 model demonstrate the feasibility of this approach. This research illustrates how LLMs can be harnessed to improve ES algorithms' performance and suggests broader applications for similar feedback loop mechanisms in various domains.

organization_info.name: Carl von Ossietzky University of Oldenburg

title: Large Language Models for Tuning Evolution Strategies

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are largely static and often redo reasoning or repeat mistakes. Prior experience reuse typically relies on external retrieval, which is similarity-based, can introduce noise, and adds latency. We introduce SEAM (Structured Experience Adapter Module), a lightweight, executor-specific plug-in that stores experience in its parameters and generates a structured, instance-tailored experience entry in a single forward pass to guide a frozen LLM executor. SEAM is trained for utility via executor rollouts and GRPO while keeping the executor frozen, and it can be further improved after deployment with supervised fine-tuning on logged successful trajectories. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains across executors with low overhead. Extensive ablations and analyses further elucidate the mechanisms underlying SEAM's effectiveness and robustness.

keyInsights: Generating utility-optimized, structured experience tailored to a specific LLM executor is more effective than retrieving superficially similar past examples.

Decoupled training allows a lightweight, pluggable module to learn how to best guide a frozen LLM, preserving the base model's general capabilities while enhancing specific task performance efficiently.
Internalizing an experience library within a generator's parameters eliminates the latency and overhead associated with external retrieval and maintenance in RAG systems.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) operate statically, often re-exploring solution paths and repeating mistakes, leading to inefficiency and a lack of adaptive learning.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely on surface-level similarity for experience retrieval, often providing low-utility or noisy guidance, and introduce substantial inference overhead and curation burden.
The challenge of enhancing LLM reasoning and task performance through experience reuse without the computational cost or risks of directly fine-tuning large, frozen models.
results: SEAM achieved state-of-the-art performance, outperforming RAG-based methods and direct executor training across four mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, MATH500, GSM8K) with two different LLM executors.

The module demonstrated significant efficiency improvements, introducing only a small, fixed per-instance latency of 0.95 seconds (compared to ~3.86-4.05 seconds for RAG) and reducing training GPU-hours substantially.

Experiments showed SEAM can provide limited cross-domain generalization, improving performance in tasks like code analysis and question answering despite being trained solely on mathematical data, and performance monotonically scaled with increased experience size.
solution: The paper introduces SEAM (Structured Experience Adapter Module), a lightweight, trainable module that internally parameterizes an experience library and generates instance-tailored, utility-optimized guidance in a single forward pass.

SEAM's generated experience follows a fixed schema, comprising problem analysis, experience highlights, and a reference plan, designed to provide diagnostic, prescriptive, and procedural steering to the frozen LLM executor.

The module is trained using a Group-Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) objective, leveraging executor rollouts and task success as a direct learning signal, while keeping the downstream LLM executor's parameters frozen.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University developed SEAM, a generative module that produces utility-optimized, structured experience for frozen large language models (LLMs), enabling them to learn from past interactions without modifying their core parameters.
